question: How do you build a global exception handler that returns structured JSON errors?
answer: Create a class annotated with @RestControllerAdvice (= @ControllerAdvice + @ResponseBody). Add @ExceptionHandler methods for each exception type you want to handle — MethodArgumentNotValidException for validation failures, EntityNotFoundException for 404s, and a catch-all Exception handler for 500s. Each handler method builds a response DTO (timestamp, status, message, field errors if applicable) and returns it. Spring serialises this to JSON. For MethodArgumentNotValidException, iterate ex.getBindingResult().getFieldErrors() to include per-field messages. Use ProblemDetail (Spring 6 / Boot 3) for RFC 9457-compliant error responses. Always return consistent shape — the client should be able to predict the error JSON structure regardless of which error occurred.
hint: @RestControllerAdvice + @ExceptionHandler methods + consistent error DTO.

question: What is Spring Data JPA and what problem does it solve?
answer: Spring Data JPA eliminates boilerplate CRUD code by generating repository implementations at runtime from interface declarations. You define an interface extending JpaRepository and Spring provides findById, save, delete, and more automatically.
hint: Auto-generated CRUD from an interface — no boilerplate implementation needed.

options.option: A SQL query builder that generates SELECT statements at compile time
Generates repository implementations from interface declarations — no boilerplate
options.comment: interface UserRepository extends JpaRepository<User, Long> — all CRUD methods are auto-implemented.

options.option: A NoSQL adapter that maps JSON documents to Java objects
A replacement for Hibernate that uses a different ORM strategy

question: What is the difference between JpaRepository and CrudRepository?
answer: CrudRepository provides basic CRUD operations. JpaRepository extends PagingAndSortingRepository (which extends CrudRepository) and adds JPA-specific methods: flush(), saveAndFlush(), deleteAllInBatch(), and findAll with Pageable/Sort.
hint: JpaRepository extends CrudRepository with pagination, sorting, and JPA-specific methods.

options.option: CrudRepository is for SQL; JpaRepository is for NoSQL
JpaRepository extends CrudRepository adding pagination and JPA batch ops
options.comment: JpaRepository is the most commonly used — it includes everything CrudRepository has plus more.

options.option: They are identical — different names for the same interface
CrudRepository is faster; JpaRepository adds security overhead

question: What is a derived query method in Spring Data?
answer: A derived query method has a name Spring Data parses to generate a JPQL query automatically. Example: findByEmailAndStatus(String email, String status) generates SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.email=? AND u.status=?
hint: Name the method with findBy, countBy, existsBy + field names — Spring generates the query.

options.option: A method annotated with @Query that derives the result type
A method Spring parses by name to generate a JPQL query automatically
options.comment: findByLastNameOrderByFirstNameAsc — finds by last name sorted by first name ascending.

options.option: A method that derives its implementation from a parent repository class
A native SQL query derived from an existing ORM entity mapping

question: What does @Transactional do in Spring?
answer: @Transactional wraps the method in a database transaction. If the method completes without an unchecked exception, the transaction commits. If an unchecked exception is thrown, it rolls back. By default, checked exceptions do NOT trigger rollback.
hint: Commits on success, rolls back on unchecked exception by default.

options.option: Makes the method thread-safe by serialising concurrent calls
Wraps in a DB transaction — commits on success, rolls back on RuntimeException
options.comment: @Transactional(rollbackFor = Exception.class) to also rollback on checked exceptions.

options.option: Validates database constraints before the method runs
Opens a new transaction even if one is already active

question: What is the N+1 query problem in JPA?
answer: N+1 occurs with lazy-loaded relationships: fetching N parent entities triggers N additional queries to load each child collection. For 100 users each with orders, that's 101 queries instead of 1. Fix: JOIN FETCH in JPQL or @EntityGraph.
hint: 1 query for parents + N queries for children — extremely inefficient at scale.

options.option: Inserting N+1 rows when only N rows were intended to be written
N parent queries + N child queries per parent — use JOIN FETCH to fix
options.comment: @EntityGraph(attributePaths='orders') on a repo method fetches orders in one JOIN query.

options.option: A bug where a query returns one extra row than expected
A loop that runs N+1 times instead of N due to an off-by-one error

question: What is the difference between lazy and eager loading in JPA?
answer: Lazy loading (default for @OneToMany) loads the related entity only when accessed. Eager loading (@ManyToOne default, or fetch=EAGER) loads it immediately with the parent. Lazy is more efficient but can cause LazyInitializationException outside a session.
hint: Lazy = load on demand; Eager = load immediately. Lazy risks LazyInitializationException.

options.option: Lazy runs before the transaction; Eager runs after commit
Lazy loads on access; Eager loads immediately with the parent
options.comment: LazyInitializationException occurs when you access a lazy collection outside an open Hibernate session.

options.option: Eager is always better — use it for all relationships
Both load at the same time — only the query plan differs

question: How do you write a custom JPQL query in Spring Data?
answer: Use @Query on the repository method with a JPQL string. JPQL references entity and field names (not table/column names). Add nativeQuery=true for a native SQL query. Use ?1, ?2 or :param named parameters.
hint: @Query with JPQL on the repository interface method.

options.option: Implement the method body directly in the interface using default methods
@Query('SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.email = :email') on the method
options.comment: JPQL: entity class names, not table names. :email is a named param matching the method parameter.

options.option: Add a @SqlQuery annotation to the entity class
Override the findAll() method in a custom repository implementation

question: What is pagination in Spring Data and how do you implement it?
answer: Pagination limits query results to a page of records. Use Pageable as a method parameter in a repository method. Spring Data executes a COUNT query and a bounded SELECT. Return Page<T> for full metadata or Slice<T> for has-next only.
hint: Pageable parameter on a repository method — returns Page<T> with total count.

options.option: Manually split the List<T> result into sublists in the service layer
Add Pageable param to the repo method and return Page<T> for metadata
options.comment: PageRequest.of(0, 20, Sort.by('name')) — page 0, 20 per page, sorted by name.

options.option: Use @Limit annotation on the entity to cap the number of results
Pagination is not supported in Spring Data — use native SQL instead

question: What is optimistic locking and how is it implemented in JPA?
answer: Optimistic locking assumes conflicts are rare — it allows concurrent reads but detects conflicts at commit time using a version column. Add @Version to a numeric or timestamp field; JPA increments it and throws OptimisticLockException if versions mismatch.
hint: @Version on a field — JPA checks it at commit and rejects stale updates.

options.option: Locks the database row on read so no other transaction can modify it
@Version field — JPA detects conflicts at commit and throws on mismatch
options.comment: Optimistic = no lock acquired; pessimistic = SELECT FOR UPDATE locks immediately on read.

options.option: Uses synchronized blocks to prevent concurrent entity access
Optimistic locking is a Spring Security feature, not a JPA feature

question: What is the N+1 problem in JPA and how do you fix it?
answer: The N+1 problem occurs when loading a collection of entities triggers a separate query for each entity's lazy-loaded relationship. Example: loading 100 orders each with a lazy Customer triggers 1 query for orders + 100 queries for customers = 101 queries total. Fixes: (1) JOIN FETCH in JPQL — SELECT o FROM Order o JOIN FETCH o.customer — fetches everything in one query. (2) @EntityGraph on a repository method — Spring Data generates the JOIN FETCH automatically. (3) Use @BatchSize on the relationship to load lazy collections in batches (less ideal but simple). (4) For DTO projections, use a constructor expression in JPQL to fetch only needed data. Avoid fetch=EAGER as a blanket fix — it always loads the relationship even when you don't need it.
hint: JOIN FETCH or @EntityGraph are the two cleanest solutions.

question: What is the difference between authentication and authorisation?
answer: Authentication verifies who you are (login with credentials). Authorisation determines what you are allowed to do (access control). You must be authenticated before authorisation can be applied.
hint: Authentication = who are you; Authorisation = what can you do.

options.option: They are synonyms — both mean verifying user identity
Authentication = who you are; Authorisation = what you can do
options.comment: Spring Security's filter chain handles authentication first, then authorisation on the resulting principal.

options.option: Authorisation happens before authentication in Spring Security
Authentication is for users; Authorisation is for services only

question: What is a JWT (JSON Web Token)?
answer: A JWT is a compact, self-contained token with three Base64-encoded parts: Header (algorithm), Payload (claims like userId, roles, expiry), and Signature (verifies integrity). The server can verify a JWT without a session store.
hint: Header.Payload.Signature — self-contained, stateless, verifiable without a DB lookup.

options.option: A session cookie that stores user data encrypted on the client
A self-contained token — header.payload.signature — verifiable without DB
options.comment: The signature is verified with the server's secret key. The payload is Base64-encoded, not encrypted.

options.option: A JSON file format for storing API credentials on the server
A Java library for generating random secure tokens

question: What is UserDetailsService in Spring Security?
answer: UserDetailsService is an interface with one method: loadUserByUsername(String username). Spring Security calls it during authentication to load user details (password hash, roles) from the database for verification.
hint: The bridge between Spring Security and your user database.

options.option: A service that creates new user accounts automatically
Spring Security calls loadUserByUsername() to fetch user from your DB
options.comment: Implement UserDetailsService in a @Service, inject your UserRepository, and return a UserDetails.

options.option: A utility class for hashing passwords before storing them
A REST endpoint that returns user details for the current session

question: What is BCryptPasswordEncoder and why should you use it?
answer: BCryptPasswordEncoder hashes passwords using bcrypt — an adaptive hash function with a configurable cost factor. It includes a salt automatically, making rainbow table attacks impossible, and the cost factor slows brute force.
hint: Adaptive hash with built-in salt — deliberately slow to prevent brute force.

options.option: A two-way encryption for storing passwords that can be decrypted
A one-way adaptive hash with built-in salt — resistant to brute force
options.comment: Never store plain-text or MD5/SHA passwords. BCrypt's cost factor can be raised as hardware gets faster.

options.option: A cipher that encrypts passwords with AES-256 for storage
A Spring Security class for encoding JWT tokens

question: What is @PreAuthorize and how does it work?
answer: @PreAuthorize evaluates a SpEL expression before the method executes. If the expression returns false, Spring throws an AccessDeniedException (403). Requires @EnableMethodSecurity on a configuration class.
hint: SpEL expression evaluated before the method — throws 403 if false.

options.option: Validates method parameters before the method is called
Evaluates a SpEL expression — throws 403 if false before method runs
options.comment: @PreAuthorize('hasRole(ADMIN)') or @PreAuthorize('hasAuthority(user:delete)').

options.option: Marks the method as requiring HTTPS to be called
Prevents the method from being called more than once per request

question: What is CSRF and when should you disable it in Spring Security?
answer: CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) protection prevents malicious sites from making requests on behalf of an authenticated browser session. It's relevant for browser/cookie-based auth. For stateless JWT APIs, CSRF protection is unnecessary — disable it.
hint: Relevant for cookie-based sessions — not needed for stateless JWT APIs.

options.option: CSRF must always be enabled — disabling it is a security vulnerability
Disable CSRF for stateless JWT REST APIs — it protects cookie sessions
options.comment: http.csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable()) in SecurityFilterChain for a stateless REST API.

options.option: CSRF is for HTTPS only — HTTP APIs don't need it
CSRF protection prevents SQL injection attacks in Spring apps

question: What is a SecurityFilterChain in Spring Security?
answer: SecurityFilterChain is a bean that configures which URL patterns require authentication, the session policy, CORS, CSRF settings, and the order of security filters. It replaced the deprecated WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter in Spring Security 5.7+.
hint: The bean that configures URL security rules and filter settings in Spring Security 5.7+.

options.option: A list of servlet filters applied to all HTTP responses
Configures URL rules, session policy, CORS, CSRF for the security layer
options.comment: @Bean SecurityFilterChain chain(HttpSecurity http) { http.authorizeHttpRequests(...).build(); }

options.option: The chain of service calls from controller to repository
A Spring Security component that validates JWT tokens

question: How does JWT-based authentication work in a Spring Boot REST API?
answer: Flow: (1) Client POSTs credentials to /auth/login. (2) Spring Security authenticates via UserDetailsService — loads the user and verifies password with BCrypt. (3) If valid, generate a JWT signed with a secret key (HMAC-SHA256 or RSA) containing userId, roles, and expiry. Return the JWT to the client. (4) On subsequent requests, the client sends the JWT in the Authorization: Bearer <token> header. (5) A custom JwtAuthenticationFilter (extends OncePerRequestFilter) intercepts each request, validates the JWT signature and expiry, extracts the user, sets an Authentication object in the SecurityContextHolder. (6) Spring Security uses the SecurityContext to enforce @PreAuthorize or URL-level access rules. Session policy is set to STATELESS — no server-side session stored.
hint: Login → JWT issued → JWT sent in Authorization header → filter validates → context set.

question: What is the difference between @SpringBootTest and @WebMvcTest?
answer: @SpringBootTest loads the full application context — all beans, real DB connection, everything. @WebMvcTest loads only the web layer (controllers, filters, security) — services and repos are not loaded and must be mocked.
hint: @SpringBootTest = full context; @WebMvcTest = controller layer only, mock the rest.

options.option: @WebMvcTest loads the full context but uses a mock HTTP server
@SpringBootTest loads everything; @WebMvcTest loads web layer only
options.comment: @WebMvcTest is faster — only controller slice. Use @MockBean for service dependencies.

options.option: @SpringBootTest is for unit tests; @WebMvcTest for integration tests
They are identical — just different annotation names

question: What is @DataJpaTest used for?
answer: @DataJpaTest loads only the JPA slice — entities, repositories, and an in-memory database (H2 by default). It doesn't load controllers or services. Each test runs in a transaction that rolls back after the test.
hint: JPA/repository slice only — uses H2 in-memory DB and auto-rollback per test.

options.option: Loads the full context to test JPA entities against a real database
Loads only JPA slice with H2 — tests roll back after each test
options.comment: @DataJpaTest + @ActiveProfiles('test') — or @AutoConfigureTestDatabase(replace=NONE) for a real DB.

options.option: Tests JPA queries against production data in a read-only transaction
Generates test data for JPA entities using factories

question: What is MockMvc and how do you use it?
answer: MockMvc performs HTTP requests against Spring MVC controllers without a real server — it's faster than starting an embedded server. Use perform() to make a request and andExpect() to assert the response status, headers, and JSON body.
hint: Simulates HTTP requests to controllers without starting a real server.

options.option: A mock version of the HTTP client used in service layer tests
Simulates HTTP to controllers — use perform() and andExpect() assertions
options.comment: mockMvc.perform(get('/users/1')).andExpect(status().isOk()).andExpect(jsonPath('$.name').value('John'))

options.option: An alternative to Postman for manual endpoint testing
A spy that records all requests for later verification

question: What is @MockBean in Spring Boot tests?
answer: @MockBean adds a Mockito mock as a Spring bean in the application context, replacing any existing bean of that type. Used in @WebMvcTest to mock the service dependencies that the controller slice doesn't load.
hint: Creates a Mockito mock and registers it as a Spring bean — replaces the real one.

options.option: Creates a mock that only works outside the Spring context
Registers a Mockito mock as a Spring bean — replaces the real bean
options.comment: @WebMvcTest + @MockBean UserService userService — the controller gets the mock injected.

options.option: A test annotation that marks a bean as optional during testing
An alias for @Mock from Mockito — they are interchangeable

question: What is Testcontainers and when should you use it?
answer: Testcontainers starts real Docker containers (Postgres, Redis, Kafka) during tests — giving you real infrastructure without H2 workarounds. Use it when you need to test against the real database engine to catch SQL dialect differences.
hint: Spins up real Docker containers for integration tests — no H2 approximations.

options.option: A library for mocking container services like Docker in unit tests
Starts real Docker containers for tests — ensures no H2 vs Postgres gaps
options.comment: @Testcontainers + @Container PostgreSQLContainer — replaces @DataJpaTest's H2 with real Postgres.

options.option: A Kubernetes tool for deploying test versions of microservices
A Spring Boot feature for running tests inside Docker containers

question: What is the difference between a unit test and an integration test?
answer: A unit test tests one class in isolation — dependencies are mocked. Fast and deterministic. An integration test loads real components together — may use a real database, HTTP client, or message broker. Slower but catches wiring and query issues.
hint: Unit = isolated with mocks; integration = real components wired together.

options.option: Integration tests only test the integration between UI and backend
Unit = one class isolated with mocks; integration = real components wired
options.comment: Aim for a test pyramid: many unit tests, fewer integration tests, even fewer end-to-end tests.

options.option: Unit tests use a real database; integration tests use H2
They are the same — the naming is just a convention

question: How do you test a service method that calls a repository using Mockito?
answer: Create the service with a mocked repository using @Mock or @ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class). Use when(repo.findById(1L)).thenReturn(Optional.of(user)) to stub the repository. Call the service method. Use verify(repo).findById(1L) to assert interactions and assertEquals/assertThat for the return value. This tests the service's business logic in complete isolation — no Spring context, no database, fast. For cases where the service should throw, use assertThrows(ExceptionType.class, () -> service.doThing(invalidInput)). For void methods, use doNothing().when(repo).delete(entity) or just verify the call happened.
hint: @ExtendWith(MockitoExtension) + @Mock repo + when(...).thenReturn(...) + verify(...).

question: What is the difference between RestTemplate and WebClient?
answer: RestTemplate is the classic synchronous HTTP client — blocks the calling thread while waiting for the response. WebClient (Spring WebFlux) is non-blocking and reactive — returns Mono/Flux. Spring recommends WebClient even in MVC apps because RestTemplate is in maintenance mode.
hint: RestTemplate = blocking; WebClient = non-blocking/reactive (preferred).

options.option: RestTemplate is newer; WebClient is the legacy REST client
RestTemplate is blocking; WebClient is non-blocking and preferred
options.comment: WebClient.create().get().uri(url).retrieve().bodyToMono(Response.class) — non-blocking async call.

options.option: WebClient only works with reactive databases — not SQL databases
They are identical — just different naming conventions

question: What is Spring Cloud OpenFeign?
answer: Feign is a declarative HTTP client — you define an interface with @FeignClient and Spring generates the HTTP implementation. Eliminates boilerplate RestTemplate/WebClient code for calling other services.
hint: Declare an interface, Spring generates the HTTP calls — no manual RestTemplate code.

options.option: A Spring Security extension for API key-based authentication
Declarative HTTP client — interface defines calls, Spring implements them
options.comment: @FeignClient(name='order-service') interface OrderClient { @GetMapping('/orders/{id}') OrderDto get(@PathVariable Long id); }

options.option: A circuit breaker implementation for inter-service calls
A reactive alternative to WebClient for blocking calls

question: What is a circuit breaker and what problem does it solve?
answer: A circuit breaker prevents a slow or failed downstream service from cascading failures to the caller. States: Closed (normal), Open (failing fast, no calls made), Half-Open (testing recovery). Resilience4j is the Spring Cloud default.
hint: Open circuit = fail fast instead of waiting; prevents cascade failures.

options.option: A retry mechanism that retries failed requests indefinitely
Opens to fail fast when a service is down — prevents cascade failures
options.comment: @CircuitBreaker(name='orderService', fallbackMethod='fallback') — from Resilience4j.

options.option: A load balancer that routes traffic away from failing instances
A database connection pool that limits concurrent DB connections

question: What is service discovery and what does Eureka provide?
answer: Service discovery allows services to find each other without hardcoded URLs. Eureka is a service registry — services register on startup and clients query it to find the current address of other services. Supports client-side load balancing.
hint: Services register in Eureka; other services look up the address instead of hardcoding it.

options.option: A Spring Boot feature for auto-discovering new REST endpoints
A registry where services register so others can find them by name
options.comment: @EnableEurekaServer for the registry; @EnableEurekaClient on each microservice.

options.option: A network scanner that finds available microservice ports automatically
A Spring Cloud component that routes all traffic through one gateway

question: What is an API gateway in a microservices architecture?
answer: An API gateway is the single entry point for all client requests. It handles cross-cutting concerns: routing to downstream services, authentication, rate limiting, SSL termination, and request aggregation. Spring Cloud Gateway is the Spring ecosystem choice.
hint: Single entry point — handles auth, routing, rate limiting before services.

options.option: A database gateway that routes SQL queries to the right schema
Single entry point handling routing, auth, rate limiting for all services
options.comment: Spring Cloud Gateway uses predicates and filters: route by path, add auth headers, rate limit by IP.

options.option: An API documentation tool that exposes Swagger for all microservices
A service that aggregates all microservice logs in one place

question: What is distributed tracing and why is it needed in microservices?
answer: Distributed tracing tracks a request as it flows across multiple services, assigning it a correlation trace ID. Without it, debugging a slow request that hits 5 services is nearly impossible. Micrometer Tracing + Zipkin/Jaeger is the Spring ecosystem approach.
hint: A correlation ID that follows a request across all services — essential for debugging.

options.option: Tracking which service version deployed to which environment
Trace IDs that follow a request across all services for end-to-end debugging
options.comment: Micrometer Tracing propagates W3C TraceContext headers; Zipkin or Jaeger visualise the traces.

options.option: Monitoring the number of instances running per service at a given time
A distributed configuration store shared across microservices

question: What is the Saga pattern for managing distributed transactions?
answer: Saga breaks a distributed transaction into a sequence of local transactions, each publishing events to trigger the next step. If a step fails, compensating transactions undo previous steps. Avoids distributed two-phase commit.
hint: Sequence of local transactions with compensating rollbacks — no distributed lock.

options.option: A two-phase commit protocol managed by Spring Cloud
Sequences of local transactions with compensating rollbacks on failure
options.comment: Choreography Saga: events trigger next steps. Orchestration Saga: a central orchestrator directs steps.

options.option: A distributed lock ensuring only one service modifies shared data
A pattern for replaying failed API calls with exponential backoff

question: What is event-driven communication between microservices?
answer: Services communicate by publishing and consuming events via a message broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ). The producer doesn't know which consumers exist — loose coupling. Enables async processing and resilience when consumers are temporarily down.
hint: Publish to a broker; consumers subscribe — loose coupling and async processing.

options.option: Services call each other directly via REST in a chain of events
Publish events to a broker; consumers subscribe — async and loosely coupled
options.comment: Spring for Apache Kafka: @KafkaListener(topics='order-created') — processes events asynchronously.

options.option: Services broadcast HTTP webhooks to registered subscriber URLs
A design where services share a single event log in a SQL database

question: Explain the circuit breaker pattern and when to use it.
answer: The circuit breaker wraps calls to an external dependency and monitors for failures. Three states: Closed (normal operation, calls pass through), Open (failure threshold exceeded — calls fail fast with a fallback, no actual calls made for a recovery window), Half-Open (after the window, a probe call tests if the dependency recovered — success closes the circuit, failure reopens it). Use it when a downstream service can be slow or unavailable and you don't want that to cascade into your service being unresponsive. Without a circuit breaker, thread pool exhaustion from waiting on slow calls makes your service appear dead to its callers. With Resilience4j in Spring Boot: @CircuitBreaker(name='inventory', fallbackMethod='inventoryFallback') — the fallback returns a default or cached response. Configure failure rate threshold, slow call threshold, and wait duration in configuration.
hint: Closed → Open (fail fast) → Half-Open (probe) — prevents cascade failures.

question: What is a multi-stage Dockerfile and why use it for Spring Boot?
answer: A multi-stage Dockerfile uses multiple FROM instructions. The build stage uses a full JDK to compile and package the app. The runtime stage uses a minimal JRE image — the compiled JAR is copied over. This produces a much smaller final image.
hint: Build with JDK, run with JRE — final image is small and has no build tools.

options.option: A Dockerfile that builds multiple microservices in one pass
Build stage with full JDK; runtime stage with minimal JRE — smaller image
options.comment: FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jdk AS build ... FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre AS runtime COPY --from=build ...

options.option: A Dockerfile that builds for multiple operating systems
Two separate Dockerfiles — one for dev and one for production

question: What is a Kubernetes Deployment and what does it manage?
answer: A Deployment manages a ReplicaSet that ensures a specified number of Pod replicas are always running. It handles rolling updates (zero-downtime), rollbacks, and self-healing (restarts failed pods).
hint: Manages replicas — rolling updates, rollbacks, and self-healing.

options.option: A Kubernetes object that stores secret configuration values
Manages Pod replicas — rolling updates, rollbacks, and self-healing
options.comment: kubectl rollout undo deployment/myapp — rolls back to the previous ReplicaSet version.

options.option: A script that deploys Docker images to multiple servers in sequence
A Kubernetes object that exposes Pods to external traffic

question: What is the difference between a Kubernetes ConfigMap and a Secret?
answer: ConfigMap stores non-sensitive configuration as key-value pairs (plain text). Secret stores sensitive data (passwords, API keys) base64-encoded and with more restricted access control. Both can be mounted as environment variables or volumes.
hint: ConfigMap = non-sensitive config; Secret = sensitive data with restricted access.

options.option: They are identical — Secret just requires a special annotation
ConfigMap = plain config; Secret = sensitive data, base64, restricted access
options.comment: Secrets are base64, not encrypted by default — use external-secrets-operator or Vault for real encryption.

options.option: ConfigMap is cluster-wide; Secret is namespace-scoped only
Secret is for TLS certificates only; ConfigMap is for app properties

question: What is the difference between a liveness probe and a readiness probe?
answer: Liveness probe checks if the container is alive — failure triggers a restart. Readiness probe checks if the container is ready to receive traffic — failure removes it from the load balancer without restarting. Both can be HTTP, TCP, or exec checks.
hint: Liveness = restart if dead; Readiness = remove from traffic if not ready.

options.option: They are the same probe — Kubernetes uses either name interchangeably
Liveness failure = restart; Readiness failure = remove from load balancer
options.comment: Startup probe is a third type — delays liveness checks while the app initialises to avoid premature restarts.

options.option: Liveness checks CPU; Readiness checks memory usage
Readiness restarts the pod; Liveness removes it from the service

question: What is a Kubernetes Service and why do Pods need it?
answer: A Kubernetes Service provides a stable DNS name and virtual IP for a group of Pods. Pods have dynamic IPs that change on restart — a Service load-balances traffic to the current healthy Pods using label selectors.
hint: Stable DNS + load balancing to Pods whose IPs change on restart.

options.option: A container inside a Pod that provides network configuration
Stable DNS and load balancing to dynamic-IP Pods via label selectors
options.comment: ClusterIP (internal), NodePort (node-level), LoadBalancer (cloud LB) — three Service types.

options.option: A Spring Boot microservice registered in Kubernetes
A daemon that monitors Pod health and restarts them on failure

question: What is a Helm chart?
answer: Helm is the Kubernetes package manager. A Helm chart is a collection of templated Kubernetes manifests with a values.yaml for configuration. helm install my-app ./chart -- with environment-specific values overrides deploys the app.
hint: Kubernetes package manager — templated manifests with configurable values.

options.option: A graph visualising Kubernetes cluster resource usage over time
Templated Kubernetes manifests managed as a deployable package
options.comment: helm upgrade --install my-app chart/ --values prod-values.yaml — installs or upgrades the release.

options.option: A Kubernetes dashboard for visualising Pod status
A CI/CD pipeline template for deploying to Kubernetes

question: What is horizontal pod autoscaling (HPA) in Kubernetes?
answer: HPA automatically scales the number of Pod replicas based on observed metrics — default is CPU utilisation, but custom metrics (requests/sec, queue depth) are also supported. It adjusts replicas between a configured min and max.
hint: Automatically adds or removes Pod replicas based on CPU or custom metrics.

options.option: Scales the CPU and memory limits of existing Pods without restarting them
Automatically adjusts replica count based on CPU or custom metrics
options.comment: kubectl autoscale deployment myapp --cpu-percent=70 --min=2 --max=10

options.option: Distributes Pods evenly across all nodes regardless of load
A Kubernetes Pod that horizontally spans multiple nodes

question: What does a GitHub Actions CI/CD workflow for a Spring Boot app typically include?
answer: A typical workflow has two stages: CI (build and test) and CD (deploy). CI: triggered on push or pull request — checks out code, sets up Java, runs mvn clean verify (compile + unit tests + integration tests), builds and pushes the Docker image to a registry (ECR, Docker Hub, GCR) tagged with the Git SHA. CD: triggered on merge to main — pulls the image just built, runs kubectl apply or helm upgrade to update the Kubernetes Deployment with the new image tag. Sensitive values (registry credentials, kubeconfig) are stored as GitHub Secrets. The workflow enforces that no unverified code is deployed — a failing test blocks the deploy. Optional additions: security scanning (Trivy), static analysis (SonarQube), environment-specific deploy gates with manual approval for production.
hint: CI = build + test + push image; CD = helm upgrade with new tag.

question: What is Spring Boot Actuator?
answer: Actuator exposes production-ready endpoints for health checks, metrics, environment properties, and application info. /actuator/health is used by Kubernetes probes; /actuator/metrics integrates with Prometheus/Micrometer.
hint: Built-in /actuator endpoints for health, metrics, and environment info.

options.option: A Spring Boot plugin for automating CRUD endpoint generation
Exposes /actuator endpoints for health, metrics, env, and app info
options.comment: management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,metrics,info — expose only what you need.

options.option: A tool for activating and deactivating Spring Profiles at runtime
A Spring Security module for actuating role-based access

question: What is @Cacheable and how does it work?
answer: @Cacheable on a method caches its return value. On first call Spring executes the method and stores the result keyed by the method arguments. Subsequent calls with the same args return the cached value without running the method body.
hint: First call executes and caches; subsequent same-arg calls skip the method body.

options.option: Marks a method's parameters as cacheable across JVM restarts
Caches return value on first call; same-arg calls return cached result
options.comment: @Cacheable('products') findProduct(Long id) — key defaults to id; use key='#id' to be explicit.

options.option: Stores the method in a compiled cache for faster JIT compilation
Enables HTTP response caching headers for REST endpoints

question: What does @Async do in Spring and what setup does it require?
answer: @Async runs a method on a separate thread from Spring's task executor instead of the caller's thread. Requires @EnableAsync on a @Configuration class. The method must return void, Future, or CompletableFuture.
hint: Runs on a thread pool thread — requires @EnableAsync on a config class.

options.option: Makes a method non-blocking by converting it to a reactive Mono
Runs on a task executor thread — requires @EnableAsync; returns Future
options.comment: The caller gets a CompletableFuture immediately; the method runs on a thread pool in parallel.

options.option: Marks a method as asynchronous in Swagger documentation
Caches the method result for asynchronous retrieval later

question: What is @Scheduled and what are its key attributes?
answer: @Scheduled runs a method on a schedule. Key attributes: fixedRate (every N ms regardless of last completion), fixedDelay (N ms after last completion), cron (cron expression for complex schedules). Requires @EnableScheduling.
hint: fixedRate = every N ms; fixedDelay = N ms after completion; cron for complex schedules.

options.option: Marks a method to only run once at application startup
Schedules a method with fixedRate, fixedDelay, or cron expression
options.comment: @Scheduled(cron='0 0 * * * *') runs at the top of every hour. @EnableScheduling must be present.

options.option: Runs a method asynchronously after a configurable startup delay
Triggers a method when a Spring event is published

question: What is Spring WebFlux and when should you choose it over Spring MVC?
answer: WebFlux is Spring's reactive web framework — non-blocking I/O using Project Reactor (Mono/Flux). Choose it when handling high concurrency with many simultaneous I/O-bound connections that would exhaust a thread-per-request model.
hint: Non-blocking I/O — use for very high concurrency where threads are the bottleneck.

options.option: Choose WebFlux for CPU-intensive batch processing tasks
Use for high-concurrency I/O-bound workloads where thread-per-request fails
options.comment: If your stack (drivers, DB) is not reactive, WebFlux loses its benefits. Spring MVC is simpler for most apps.

options.option: WebFlux is always faster than Spring MVC — use it for all projects
WebFlux replaces Spring MVC and cannot run alongside it

question: What is a custom HealthIndicator in Spring Boot Actuator?
answer: Implement HealthIndicator and override health() to return Health.up() or Health.down() with custom details. Spring Actuator includes your indicator in the /actuator/health response — useful for checking external dependencies like queues or APIs.
hint: Implement HealthIndicator — Actuator includes it in the /actuator/health response.

options.option: A custom Kubernetes probe that checks /health every 30 seconds
Implements HealthIndicator to add custom checks to /actuator/health
options.comment: health().withDetail('queueDepth', 5).withStatus(Status.UP).build() — included in health response.

options.option: A class that monitors JVM health and writes to a log file
An annotation that marks endpoints as healthy for load balancers

question: What are Spring Profiles and how do they enable environment-specific configuration?
answer: Profiles activate a named configuration context. application-dev.yml, application-prod.yml contain environment-specific values (DB URLs, log levels). @Profile('dev') on a @Bean activates it only in the dev profile. Activate via spring.profiles.active.
hint: Named config files activated by environment — different DB, logs, features per env.

options.option: A performance profiling tool built into Spring Boot
Named envs with separate config files — @Profile limits beans to that env
options.comment: @Profile('!prod') on a dev-only mock bean ensures it never runs in production.

options.option: A Spring Security feature for assigning roles based on user profile
A set of JVM flags applied per environment during startup

question: What is Spring Boot DevTools and what does it provide?
answer: DevTools enhances the development experience: automatic application restart when classes change, LiveReload for browser refresh, and default dev-friendly settings (caching disabled, detailed error pages). It is excluded from production JARs.
hint: Auto-restart on class change + LiveReload — only active in development.

options.option: A debugging toolkit for inspecting Spring beans at runtime in production
Auto-restarts on class change + LiveReload — excluded from production
options.comment: Add spring-boot-devtools to the classpath in dev only — it detects it's in a fat JAR and deactivates.

options.option: A Spring plugin that generates REST API documentation automatically
A tool for remote debugging Spring Boot apps on Kubernetes

question: When should you choose Spring WebFlux over Spring MVC for a new service?
answer: Choose WebFlux when the service is predominantly I/O-bound with high concurrency — thousands of simultaneous long-lived connections (WebSocket, SSE), or proxying/aggregating many upstream service calls where threads would be wasted waiting. In a thread-per-request model (MVC), high concurrency exhausts the thread pool; WebFlux handles the same load with fewer threads by using non-blocking I/O and event loops. Caveats: your entire stack must be reactive — if you use blocking drivers (JDBC, legacy libs), WebFlux doesn't help and may be harmful. Spring MVC is simpler, better supported by the tooling and developer community, and handles most standard web apps fine. For a service with moderate traffic, database CRUD, and simple request/response patterns, Spring MVC is the right default. Only reach for WebFlux when you have a specific high-concurrency I/O problem that benchmarking shows MVC can't handle.
hint: High-concurrency I/O-bound = WebFlux; standard CRUD with moderate traffic = Spring MVC.

question: What is React?
answer: React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces. It was created by Facebook (Meta) and focuses on the view layer — building reusable UI components that update efficiently when data changes.
hint: It's a library, not a framework — it only handles the UI layer.

options.option: A backend framework for building REST APIs in JavaScript
A JavaScript library for building user interfaces
options.comment: React handles the V in MVC — you add routing, state management, and data fetching separately.

options.option: A CSS framework for building responsive layouts
A database ORM for JavaScript applications

question: What is the Virtual DOM?
answer: The Virtual DOM is a lightweight in-memory representation of the real DOM. React updates the Virtual DOM first, diffs it against the previous version, then applies only the changed parts to the real DOM — minimising expensive DOM operations.
hint: React's performance trick — update a cheap copy first, then patch only the differences.

options.option: A server-side copy of the DOM for SEO rendering
An in-memory DOM copy React diffs to minimise real DOM updates
options.comment: Real DOM manipulation is slow. React batches and minimises it using the Virtual DOM diff algorithm.

options.option: A virtual machine that runs React components in isolation
A browser extension that speeds up React development

question: What does 'declarative' mean in the context of React?
answer: Declarative means you describe what the UI should look like for a given state, and React handles the DOM updates to make it so. You don't imperatively manipulate the DOM — React reconciles it for you.
hint: You describe the desired end state; React figures out how to get there.

options.option: You write step-by-step instructions to manipulate the DOM directly
You describe what UI should look like; React handles the DOM changes
options.comment: Contrast with jQuery: declarative React vs imperative DOM manipulation with document.getElementById.

options.option: You declare all components at the top of the file before using them
React generates HTML declarations from JavaScript at build time

question: What is a React component?
answer: A React component is a reusable, self-contained piece of UI. It is a JavaScript function (or class) that accepts props and returns JSX describing what should appear on screen.
hint: A function that takes props and returns JSX.

options.option: A CSS class that styles a section of the web page
A reusable function that accepts props and returns JSX
options.comment: Components are the fundamental building blocks of React apps — compose them to build complex UIs.

options.option: A configuration file that sets up a React application
A JavaScript module that handles HTTP requests

question: What is the difference between React and a full framework like Angular?
answer: React is a UI library — it only handles rendering. You choose your own routing, state management, and HTTP libraries. Angular is a complete framework with routing, forms, HTTP, and DI built in and opinionated conventions.
hint: React is 'bring your own everything'; Angular is batteries-included.

options.option: React and Angular are identical — just from different companies
React is a UI library; Angular is a complete framework with built-in tools
options.comment: React's flexibility means more choices; Angular's opinionation means faster team onboarding.

options.option: Angular is only for mobile; React is only for web
React requires TypeScript; Angular works with JavaScript only

question: What company created React and when was it first released publicly?
answer: React was created by Facebook (now Meta) and open-sourced in 2013. Jordan Walke initially built it to solve Facebook's news feed performance problems.
hint: Facebook open-sourced it — the year starts with 201.

options.option: Google, released in 2010
Meta (Facebook), open-sourced in 2013
options.comment: React was first used internally at Facebook in 2011, then open-sourced at JSConf US in May 2013.

options.option: Microsoft, released in 2015
Twitter, released in 2012

question: What is React's reconciliation?
answer: Reconciliation is React's process of comparing the new Virtual DOM tree with the previous one (diffing) and computing the minimal set of real DOM changes needed to bring the UI up to date.
hint: The diff algorithm that figures out what actually changed between renders.

options.option: The process of merging two React components into one
Diffing the new vs old Virtual DOM to compute minimal DOM updates
options.comment: The key heuristic: components of the same type at the same position are updated, not replaced.

options.option: Syncing React state with a backend database
Resolving prop conflicts between parent and child components

question: Why does React use a component-based architecture?
answer: Component-based architecture breaks the UI into small, self-contained, reusable pieces. Benefits: reusability (write a Button once, use it everywhere), encapsulation (component manages its own state and styles), composability (combine simple components to build complex UIs), and maintainability (change one component without affecting others). Each component is a function — easy to read, test, and reason about in isolation.
hint: Think reusability, encapsulation, and composability.

question: What does Create React App (CRA) do?
answer: CRA is an officially supported CLI tool that scaffolds a new React project with Webpack, Babel, ESLint, and a dev server pre-configured — so you can start writing components immediately without any build configuration.
hint: Zero-config React starter — Webpack and Babel are set up for you.

options.option: A React component library with pre-built UI elements
A CLI that scaffolds a React app with Webpack and Babel pre-configured
options.comment: npx create-react-app my-app — though Vite is now the recommended faster alternative.

options.option: A tool that converts Angular projects to React automatically
A browser extension for debugging React applications

question: Why is Vite preferred over Create React App for new React projects?
answer: Vite uses native ES modules in the browser during development — no bundling required. This makes cold starts nearly instant and HMR extremely fast. CRA uses Webpack which bundles everything upfront, making it slow on large projects.
hint: Vite serves files as native ES modules — no bundling during dev.

options.option: Vite includes a built-in database and backend server
Vite uses native ES modules for instant dev starts and fast HMR
options.comment: npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react — the modern way to start a React project.

options.option: Vite is preferred because it uses a different JavaScript syntax
Vite automatically deploys your app to the cloud on save

question: What is the role of index.html in a React application?
answer: index.html is the single HTML file served for all routes. It contains a <div id="root"> where React mounts the entire application. React replaces that div's contents at runtime — everything else is rendered by JavaScript.
hint: It has one div — id='root' — where React mounts the whole app.

options.option: Each page in a React app has its own index.html file
The single HTML shell with a root div where React mounts the app
options.comment: React is a Single Page Application (SPA) — one HTML file, JavaScript handles all navigation.

options.option: It defines all React components and their rendering order
It is only used during development and excluded from production

question: What does ReactDOM.createRoot() do?
answer: createRoot() (React 18+) creates a React root attached to a DOM node and enables concurrent features. You call root.render(<App />) to mount the React component tree into the page.
hint: React 18's way to mount your app — enables concurrent rendering.

options.option: Creates a new HTML document for each React component
Creates a React root on a DOM node and enables concurrent features
options.comment: Replaced ReactDOM.render() in React 18. root.render(<App />) mounts the app.

options.option: Generates the root component of a React application automatically
Creates a virtual root node separate from the real DOM

question: What is Hot Module Replacement (HMR) in a React dev server?
answer: HMR updates changed modules in the browser instantly without a full page reload. React component changes appear immediately while preserving application state — making development feedback loops very fast.
hint: Updates code in the browser without refreshing — state is preserved.

options.option: A feature that replaces deprecated React APIs automatically
Updates changed modules live in the browser without a full reload
options.comment: Vite's HMR is nearly instant; CRA's Webpack HMR is slower on large projects.

options.option: A hot key that triggers a manual browser reload during development
A system that swaps React versions at runtime without reinstalling

question: What is the purpose of the public folder in a React project?
answer: The public folder contains static assets served as-is without processing by the bundler — favicon, robots.txt, and images referenced directly by URL. index.html lives here in CRA projects.
hint: Static files served without bundling — not processed by Webpack/Vite.

options.option: All React components must be placed in the public folder
Static assets served without bundling — favicon, index.html, robots.txt
options.comment: Files in src/ are bundled; files in public/ are copied as-is. Reference them with %PUBLIC_URL%/file.

options.option: The public folder is where API routes are defined in React
It stores the compiled output of the React build process

question: What are React Developer Tools used for?
answer: React Developer Tools is a browser extension (Chrome/Firefox) that lets you inspect the React component tree, view and edit props and state in real time, and profile component render performance.
hint: Browser extension for inspecting component trees and profiling renders.

options.option: A command-line tool for scaffolding new React components
A browser extension to inspect component trees and profile renders
options.comment: Open DevTools → Components tab to browse the React tree; Profiler tab to find slow renders.

options.option: A testing framework for writing unit tests in React
A tool that automatically fixes React code style issues

question: What is JSX?
answer: JSX (JavaScript XML) is a syntax extension for JavaScript that looks like HTML but compiles to React.createElement() calls. It lets you write UI structure in a familiar HTML-like syntax directly inside JavaScript.
hint: HTML-like syntax in JavaScript — Babel compiles it to createElement() calls.

options.option: A new programming language created by the React team
A syntax extension that compiles to React.createElement() calls
options.comment:
Hi
compiles to React.createElement('div', {className:'box'}, 'Hi').

options.option: A JSON format for describing React component structure
A CSS preprocessor used exclusively in React projects

question: What does Babel do with JSX in a React project?
answer: Babel is a JavaScript compiler that transforms JSX syntax into plain JavaScript React.createElement() calls that browsers can execute. Without Babel (or the new JSX transform), browsers cannot parse JSX directly.
hint: Compiles JSX → React.createElement() so browsers can run it.

options.option: Babel bundles all React files into one JavaScript file
Babel compiles JSX to React.createElement() calls browsers understand
options.comment: React 17+ introduced the new JSX transform — no longer needs import React in every file.

options.option: Babel checks JSX for type errors at compile time
Babel converts JSX directly to HTML for server-side rendering

question: Why must JSX return a single root element?
answer: JSX compiles to a single React.createElement() call which returns one value. A function cannot return two values, so JSX must have one root. Wrap siblings in a <div> or a Fragment (<>) to avoid adding extra DOM nodes.
hint: A function returns one value — JSX compiles to one createElement call.

options.option: It is a browser limitation — the DOM only accepts one root element
JSX compiles to one function call which can only return one value
options.comment: Use <React.Fragment> or shorthand <> </> to wrap siblings without adding a real DOM node.

options.option: React only renders the first element and ignores the rest silently
It is a style convention — technically multiple roots are allowed

question: What is a React Fragment and when do you use it?
answer: A Fragment (<React.Fragment> or shorthand <>) lets you group multiple JSX elements under a single root without adding an extra DOM node. Use it when wrapping in a div would break styling or DOM semantics.
hint: Groups siblings without adding a real DOM node — shorthand is <>...</>.

options.option: A fragment that stores a reusable piece of HTML for later use
Groups multiple elements without adding an extra DOM node
options.comment:
<>
  • One
  • Two
    • — renders two list items with no wrapper div.

options.option: A performance feature that lazy-loads parts of a component
A React error boundary that catches rendering failures

question: How do you embed a JavaScript expression inside JSX?
answer: Wrap the expression in curly braces {}. Expressions that produce a value are allowed: variables, function calls, ternary operators, array.map(). Statements like if/for are not allowed directly in JSX.
hint: Wrap the value-producing expression in curly braces {}.

options.option: Use double curly braces {{ }} for all JavaScript in JSX
Wrap any value-producing expression in single curly braces {}
options.comment:
{user.name}

or

{isLoggedIn ? 'Hi' : 'Login'}

— expressions only, not statements.

options.option: Prefix the expression with $ like in template literals
Use <script> tags inside JSX to embed JavaScript

question: Why do you use className instead of class in JSX?
answer: class is a reserved keyword in JavaScript. Since JSX is JavaScript, you must use className instead. Similarly, for becomes htmlFor on label elements. React maps these to the correct HTML attributes in the DOM.
hint: class is a JS reserved word — JSX uses className instead.

options.option: React invented a new attribute system incompatible with HTML
class is a reserved JS keyword so JSX uses className instead
options.comment: React maps className → class and htmlFor → for when rendering to the real DOM.

options.option: className adds extra React-specific functionality beyond CSS class
Both class and className work — className is just the convention

question: What is the difference between a React element and a React component?
answer: A React element is a plain object describing what to render — the output of JSX or React.createElement(). A component is a function or class that produces elements. Components are called; elements are the return value.
hint: Element = the description (object); Component = the function that returns it.

options.option: They are the same thing — React uses the terms interchangeably
Element = plain object describing UI; Component = function that returns elements
options.comment: <Button /> is a component call; the returned <button>Click</button> is an element.

options.option: Elements are for HTML tags; components are for custom logic only
A component can only contain one element

question: Explain what JSX compiles to and why that matters.
answer:
JSX compiles to React.createElement(type, props, ...children) calls. For example,

Hello

becomes React.createElement('h1', { className: 'title' }, 'Hello'). This returns a plain JavaScript object — a React element — describing the node type, its props, and children. This matters because: (1) it shows JSX is just syntactic sugar, not magic; (2) you can write React without JSX if needed; (3) understanding the compiled output helps debug prop passing and key issues; (4) React 17+ introduced a new JSX transform that no longer requires importing React in every file — Babel/Vite handle the createElement import automatically.
hint: JSX → React.createElement() → plain JS object describing the UI.

question: What is the difference between a functional and a class component?
answer: A functional component is a plain JavaScript function that returns JSX. A class component extends React.Component and implements a render() method. Since React Hooks (v16.8), functional components can do everything class components can — they are the modern standard.
hint: Functional = plain function; class = extends React.Component. Hooks made classes obsolete.

options.option: Class components are faster; functional components are for simple UIs
Functional components are the modern standard; class components are legacy
options.comment: Hooks give functional components state and lifecycle. New code should always use functional components.

options.option: Functional components cannot use state — only class components can
They are identical — the names are just style preferences

question: What are props in React?
answer: Props (properties) are read-only data passed from a parent component to a child component. They are received as the first argument of a functional component. Props flow one way — parent to child.
hint: Read-only data passed from parent to child — one-way data flow.

options.option: Mutable values a component manages and updates internally
Read-only data passed from parent to child — one-way data flow
options.comment:
function Greeting({ name }) { return

Hello {name}

; } — name is a prop.

options.option: CSS properties applied to a React component's root element
Built-in React methods like render() and setState()

question: What is props.children?
answer: props.children contains whatever content is nested between a component's opening and closing tags. It lets you build wrapper or layout components that don't know their content ahead of time.
hint: Whatever you put between <Component> and </Component> tags.

options.option: An array of all child components defined in the same file
The JSX nested between a component's opening and closing tags
options.comment:
Content

 — inside Card, props.children is the

element.

options.option: A list of all HTML elements rendered by a component
The sub-components explicitly imported into a file

question: What is prop drilling and why is it a problem?
answer: Prop drilling is passing props through multiple intermediate components that don't use them, just to reach a deeply nested component that does. It makes components unnecessarily coupled and refactoring painful. Context API or state management libraries solve this.
hint: Passing props through components that don't need them — just to reach a deep child.

options.option: Drilling into the DOM to inspect prop values in DevTools
Passing props through intermediaries that don't use them — leads to coupling
options.comment: Solve with Context API for global state, or component composition to avoid unnecessary intermediaries.

options.option: A performance technique for passing large data structures via props
Using TypeScript to validate prop types at compile time

question: What is PropTypes in React?
answer: PropTypes is a runtime type-checking library for React props. You define expected types and required props; React logs warnings in development if the wrong type is passed. TypeScript is the modern alternative for compile-time checking.
hint: Runtime prop type validation — logs dev warnings when types mismatch.

options.option: A TypeScript utility that automatically infers prop types
Runtime prop validation that logs dev warnings for type mismatches
options.comment: Button.propTypes = { label: PropTypes.string.isRequired } — TypeScript is preferred in new projects.

options.option: A list of built-in React props like className and onClick
A compiler plugin that removes prop types from production builds

question: What are default props and how do you define them?
answer: Default props provide fallback values when a prop is not passed. In functional components, use default parameter values: function Button({ color = 'blue' }) {}. In class components, use Component.defaultProps = {}.
hint: Use default parameter values in functional component signatures.

options.option: Props automatically generated by React for every component
Fallback values for optional props — set via default parameters or defaultProps
options.comment: function Card({ title = 'Untitled', size = 'md' }) {} — modern approach using JS defaults.

options.option: The first props passed to a component when it is created
Props that cannot be changed by the parent component

question: What does it mean that props are immutable in React?
answer: A component must never modify its own props. Props are owned by the parent — if the child needs to affect the parent's data, it does so through a callback function passed as a prop. Mutating props breaks React's one-way data flow.
hint: Children read props; they never write to them. Callbacks go upward.

options.option: Props are frozen objects — JavaScript throws if you try to write them
Props are owned by the parent — child must use callbacks to affect parent
options.comment: Mutating props() directly would cause bugs since the parent controls its own state, not the child.

options.option: Immutable props means they must be strings or numbers only
Immutability means props cannot contain functions or objects

question: Explain the difference between props and state in React.
answer: Props are external — passed from parent to child, read-only inside the component, and controlled by whoever renders the component. State is internal — owned and managed by the component itself, mutable via setState/useState, and triggers a re-render when changed. A component cannot change its own props, but it can change its own state. Think of props as the function arguments and state as local variables inside the function. When a component needs to communicate upward (child → parent), it does so by calling a callback function passed down as a prop.
hint: Props = external/read-only; State = internal/mutable. Each triggers re-renders when changed.

question: What is useState and what does it return?
answer: useState is a React Hook that adds state to a functional component. It returns a tuple: [currentValue, setterFunction]. The setter triggers a re-render with the new value.
hint: Returns [value, setter] — calling setter triggers a re-render.

options.option: A hook that returns the current URL state of the application
Returns [currentValue, setter] — setter triggers a re-render
options.comment: const [count, setCount] = useState(0) — 0 is the initial value; setCount(1) triggers re-render.

options.option: A function that returns the global state of the entire app
A hook that saves state to localStorage automatically

question: What happens when you call a state setter function in React?
answer: Calling a state setter schedules a re-render of the component. React batches multiple state updates in the same event handler and re-renders once. The new state value is available in the next render, not immediately after the call.
hint: It schedules a re-render — the new value is not available until the next render.

options.option: State updates immediately — you can read the new value on the next line
Schedules a re-render — new value available in the next render cycle
options.comment: setCount(count + 1); console.log(count) — still logs the old value. Re-render happens asynchronously.

options.option: It re-renders all components in the entire application tree
It saves the value to the database and then triggers a reload

question: Why should you never mutate state directly in React?
answer: Direct mutation doesn't trigger a re-render because React compares object references. If you mutate an object in place, the reference stays the same and React sees no change. Always create a new object/array with the updated value.
hint: React compares references — mutation leaves the reference unchanged, so no re-render.

options.option: React will throw an error if you mutate state directly
Mutation keeps the same reference so React sees no change — no re-render
options.comment: arr.push(item) mutates. Do [...arr, item] to create a new array reference React will detect.

options.option: Mutating state directly causes the entire app to crash
It is allowed in class components but not functional components

question: What is the functional update form of useState?
answer: Instead of setCount(count + 1), use setCount(prev => prev + 1). This functional form receives the latest state value as its argument, preventing stale closure bugs when multiple updates are batched or when the setter is called in async code.
hint: setCount(prev => prev + 1) — always gets the latest value, not a stale closure.

options.option: A way to reset state to its initial value in one call
setCount(prev => prev + 1) — receives latest state to avoid stale closures
options.comment: Critical inside useEffect or async handlers where the captured count may be stale.

options.option: A method that updates state synchronously instead of asynchronously
A pattern for updating multiple state values in one useState call

question: What is useEffect used for?
answer: useEffect runs side effects after render — data fetching, subscriptions, DOM manipulation, timers, and logging. It replaces componentDidMount, componentDidUpdate, and componentWillUnmount from class components.
hint: Side effects after render — fetch data, subscribe, set up timers.

options.option: Runs code before the component renders for the first time
Runs side effects after render — fetch, subscribe, DOM manipulation
options.comment: useEffect replaces componentDidMount + componentDidUpdate + componentWillUnmount in one hook.

options.option: Measures the render duration and logs it to the console
Prevents a component from re-rendering when props change

question: What does the dependency array in useEffect control?
answer: The dependency array controls when the effect re-runs. Empty [] means run once after mount. [value] means re-run whenever value changes. No array at all means re-run after every render.
hint: [] = once on mount; [dep] = on dep change; no array = every render.

options.option: Lists the state variables that the effect is allowed to read
[] = once; [dep] = on dep change; omitted = after every render
options.comment: Missing a dependency in the array is a common bug — use the exhaustive-deps ESLint rule.

options.option: Specifies the order in which multiple effects run
Controls how many times the effect runs in total

question: What is a cleanup function in useEffect and when is it needed?
answer: Return a function from useEffect to run cleanup before the next effect or before the component unmounts. Required for: clearing timers (clearInterval), unsubscribing (EventEmitter.off), cancelling fetch (AbortController). Prevents memory leaks.
hint: Return a function from useEffect — it runs on unmount or before re-running.

options.option: A function that clears all state when a component unmounts
Return a function to clean up subscriptions, timers, and fetch on unmount
options.comment: useEffect(() => { const id = setInterval(fn, 1000); return () => clearInterval(id); }, []);

options.option: A callback that runs before the component mounts for setup tasks

question: What is state lifting in React?
answer: Lifting state up means moving state to the closest common ancestor of the components that need to share it. The ancestor manages the state and passes it down as props — the standard pattern before Context or Redux.
hint: Move shared state to the nearest common ancestor and pass it down as props.

options.option: Moving state to a global Redux store for performance
Moving shared state to the nearest common ancestor component
options.comment: If siblings A and B both need the same value, lift it to their parent and pass down as props.

options.option: Converting state from a hook to a class component

question: Explain how useEffect with a cleanup function works during a component's lifecycle.
answer: When a component mounts, useEffect runs after the first render. If the effect returns a cleanup function, React stores it. When the component re-renders and the dependencies changed, React runs the previous cleanup first, then the new effect. When the component unmounts, React runs the cleanup one final time. This mirrors the class lifecycle: setup runs like componentDidMount/componentDidUpdate; cleanup runs like componentWillUnmount. Example: subscribing to a WebSocket — the effect opens the connection, the cleanup closes it. Without cleanup, a component that unmounts while waiting for a fetch can try to update state on an unmounted component, causing memory leaks and React warnings.
hint: Mount → effect; deps change → cleanup then effect; unmount → cleanup.

question: How do you attach a click handler to a button in React?
answer: Use the onClick prop with a function reference: <button onClick={handleClick}>. Never call the function inline with parentheses — onClick={handleClick()} would call it immediately during render, not on click.
hint: onClick={fn} — no parentheses, or it runs during render not on click.

options.option: <button onclick='handleClick()'>Click</button>
<button onClick={handleClick}>Click</button>
options.comment: React uses camelCase event names (onClick, not onclick). Pass a reference, not a call.

options.option: <button onClick={handleClick()}>Click</button>

question: What is a synthetic event in React?
answer: A synthetic event is React's cross-browser wrapper around the native browser event. It normalises event properties so they behave consistently across browsers. It exposes the same API as native events including preventDefault() and stopPropagation().
hint: A cross-browser wrapper around the native event — same API, consistent behaviour.

question: What does e.preventDefault() do in a React form handler?
answer: e.preventDefault() prevents the default browser action. In a form submit handler, it stops the browser from making an HTTP POST and reloading the page — allowing React to handle the submission instead.

question: What is the difference between e.preventDefault() and e.stopPropagation()?
answer: e.preventDefault() stops the browser's default action (form submit, link navigation). e.stopPropagation() stops the event from bubbling up to parent elements. They are independent — you can call both if needed.

question: What are the three main ways to do conditional rendering in React?
answer: 1. if/else outside the return — assign JSX to a variable. 2. Ternary in JSX: {isLoggedIn ? <Dashboard /> : <Login />}. 3. Short-circuit &&: {isLoggedIn && <Dashboard />} — renders Dashboard only if true.

question: What is the && short-circuit pitfall with numbers in JSX?
answer: {count && <Item />} renders the number 0 when count is 0 — because 0 is falsy but JSX renders it as a text node. Fix: use {count > 0 && <Item />} or a ternary {count ? <Item /> : null}.

question: How do you pass parameters to an event handler in React?
answer: Use an arrow function: onClick={() => handleDelete(item.id)}. The arrow function is created fresh each render but only calls handleDelete with the argument when clicked. Avoid onClick={handleDelete(item.id)} as that calls it immediately.

question: When would you use a ternary versus && for conditional rendering?
answer: Use the ternary operator when you need to render one of two things: {isLoggedIn ? <Dashboard /> : <Login />}. Use && when you only want to show something or show nothing: {hasNotifications && <Badge count={n} />}. Avoid && with number values because 0 renders as text — always compare explicitly: {items.length > 0 && <List />}. For complex conditions, extract the logic into a variable before the return statement using if/else — this keeps JSX clean and readable. Never use if statements directly inside JSX since JSX only supports expressions.
hint: Ternary = one of two; && = show or nothing. Watch the number pitfall.

question: How do you render a list of items in React?

  • {item.name}

question: What is the key prop and what does React use it for?
answer: The key prop is a unique identifier React uses to track list items across renders. When the list changes, React matches items by key to decide what to update, move, or unmount — making list re-renders efficient.
hint: React uses keys to match items between renders — determines what to update vs recreate.

options.comment: Key must be unique among siblings — not globally. React does not expose key as a prop inside the child.

question: Why is using the array index as a key a problem?
answer: When items are reordered, added, or removed, index-based keys reassign the wrong key to each item. React thinks the item at index 0 is always the same item — causing incorrect state, animations, and rendering bugs.
hint: If the list reorders or filters, indices shift — React matches the wrong items.

question: What warning does React log when keys are missing from list items?
answer: React logs: 'Each child in a list should have a unique key prop.' in the browser console during development. Missing keys force React to re-render all list items on every change instead of only the changed ones.

question: Can two sibling list items have the same key?
answer: No. Keys must be unique among siblings in the same list. Duplicate keys cause React to behave unpredictably and log a warning. Keys don't need to be globally unique — only among siblings at the same level.

options.comment: Keys are scoped per-list. The same key value can be used in two different lists without conflict.

question: What should you use as a key when the data has no unique ID?
answer: Generate a stable unique ID on creation (UUID, nanoid) and store it with the data. Never generate keys during render (Math.random(), Date.now()) — they change every render, destroying component state. Index is acceptable only for static, never-reordered lists.
hint: Generate a stable ID when creating the data — never generate during render.

options.option: Use Math.random() to generate a unique key each render

question: Explain why React needs keys on list items and what goes wrong without them.
answer: React's reconciliation algorithm needs to match elements between the previous and new render to decide what to update. For static elements it uses position, but for lists the order can change. Keys give React a stable identity for each item regardless of position. Without keys, React uses index by default — meaning if you prepend an item to a list, every item after it appears to have changed (its index key changed), causing React to re-render and re-mount all of them unnecessarily. With correct keys (stable IDs), React identifies that the existing items just moved and only mounts the new item. Visible symptoms of missing or wrong keys: input values shifting to wrong items when the list reorders, animation glitches, and unnecessary unmount/remount of components losing their internal state.
hint: Keys = stable identity across re-renders. Without them, React can't tell moved from new.

question: What is a controlled component in React?
answer: A controlled component has its form input value driven by React state. The value prop is set from state and onChange updates the state. React is the single source of truth — the DOM value always mirrors the state.

question: What is an uncontrolled component and when would you use one?
answer: An uncontrolled component lets the DOM manage its own state. You use a ref to read the value only when needed (e.g. on submit) instead of tracking every keystroke. Useful for file inputs and when integrating with non-React code.
hint: DOM owns the value; read it with a ref only when needed.

question: What is useRef used for in form handling?

question: How do you handle a textarea in React versus plain HTML?
answer: In HTML, textarea content goes between tags: <textarea>content</textarea>. In React, it's a self-closing element with a value prop like an input: <textarea value={text} onChange={e => setText(e.target.value)} />.

question: What is the difference between onChange and onBlur on a form input?
answer: onChange fires on every keystroke as the value changes. onBlur fires when the input loses focus. Use onChange for live validation and controlled inputs; use onBlur for validation that should only trigger after the user finishes typing.
