options.comment: For recursive listing use fs.readdirSync recursively or the glob npm package.

options.option: fs.scandir(path) — scans all files recursively including subdirectories
os.listdir(path) — the correct module for listing directory contents

question: How do you make an HTTP GET request in Node.js without npm packages?
answer: Use the built-in http (or https) module: http.get(url, (res) => { let data = ''; res.on('data', chunk => data += chunk); res.on('end', () => console.log(data)); }). The response is a stream — you accumulate chunks. For modern code, prefer the fetch API (built into Node.js since v18) or the node-fetch/axios npm packages.

question: What is the difference between http and https modules in Node.js?
answer: http handles plain unencrypted HTTP traffic. https handles encrypted TLS/SSL traffic. To create an HTTPS server you need an SSL certificate and key: https.createServer({ key, cert }, handler).listen(443). When making outbound requests, use https.get() for HTTPS URLs and http.get() for HTTP URLs.
hint: Same API — https adds TLS encryption and requires a certificate and key.

https adds TLS encryption — needs a certificate + key to create a server
options.comment: In production, most Node.js apps terminate TLS at a reverse proxy (nginx) not in Node itself.

https module is only available on Linux — use http on all platforms

question: What is fs.watch() used for in Node.js?
answer: fs.watch(path, callback) watches a file or directory for changes. The callback receives the event type ('rename' or 'change') and the filename. It's used to rebuild or reload when files change — for example, tools like nodemon use file watchers to restart a Node.js server when source files are modified.
hint: Watches a file or directory for changes — used by tools like nodemon to auto-restart.

options.option: Locks a file to prevent other processes from writing to it concurrently
Watches a file or directory for changes — fires callback on rename/change
options.comment: fs.watch has OS-level quirks; the chokidar npm package is more reliable cross-platform.

Monitors filesystem disk usage and alerts when a threshold is exceeded

question: Explain the role of the Node.js http module and how a request-response cycle works.
answer: The http module is Node.js's built-in way to create web servers and make HTTP requests. When you call http.createServer(handler), Node.js starts listening for TCP connections on the specified port. Each incoming HTTP request triggers the handler with two objects: req (IncomingMessage) containing the method, URL, headers, and body stream — and res (ServerResponse) used to send back status, headers, and body. The body of a POST request is streamed — you must collect data events and parse them. Response flow: res.writeHead(200, headers) sets status and headers; res.write(chunk) sends body chunks; res.end() closes the response. Express is built on top of this, adding routing, middleware, and body parsing so you don't write the raw plumbing yourself.
hint: http.createServer gets req (method, URL, headers, body stream) and res (status, headers, body).

question: What is the EventEmitter class in Node.js?
answer: EventEmitter is a core Node.js class (from the events module) that implements the observer pattern. Any object can extend it to emit named events and allow listeners to subscribe. Key methods: .on(event, listener) to subscribe, .emit(event, ...args) to fire, .off() or .removeListener() to unsubscribe, .once() for one-time listeners.
hint: Core class for the observer pattern — emit events, subscribe with .on(), unsubscribe with .off().

options.option: A browser DOM event system imported into Node.js for compatibility
Core class for pub/sub — .on() to subscribe, .emit() to fire events
options.comment: Many Node.js core objects (http.Server, fs.ReadStream) extend EventEmitter under the hood.

options.option: A module that listens for incoming HTTP events and routes them to handlers
A scheduler that fires callbacks at fixed intervals using event triggers

question: What is the difference between .on() and .once() in EventEmitter?
answer: .on(event, listener) registers a listener that fires every time the event is emitted. .once(event, listener) registers a listener that fires only the first time the event is emitted, then automatically removes itself. Use .once() for one-time setup events like 'connect' or 'ready'.
hint: .on() fires every time; .once() fires only once then removes itself automatically.

options.comment: Using .on() when you wanted .once() is a common source of memory leaks in Node.js.

options.option: .once() fires immediately at registration; .on() fires on future events

question: What is a Readable stream in Node.js?
answer: A Readable stream is an abstraction for consuming data from a source piece by piece — without loading it all into memory. Sources include files (fs.createReadStream), HTTP request bodies, stdin, and database query result sets. Data flows via 'data' events in flowing mode, or you can read chunks manually with .read().
hint: A source of data consumed in chunks — files, HTTP request bodies, stdin.

options.option: A synchronous function that reads a file and returns the full content
An abstraction for consuming data in chunks — files, HTTP body, stdin
options.comment: Readable streams allow you to process a 10GB file with constant, low memory usage.

options.option: A browser Web API polyfilled in Node.js for reading DOM elements
A network socket that only receives data and cannot send responses

question: What is the purpose of the pipe() method in Node.js streams?
answer: pipe() connects a Readable stream to a Writable stream, automatically forwarding data chunks from the source to the destination and handling backpressure. Example: fs.createReadStream('input.mp4').pipe(fs.createWriteStream('output.mp4')). This copies a file in streaming fashion — no matter how large, memory stays constant.
hint: Connects a Readable to a Writable stream — handles data forwarding and backpressure automatically.

question: What is backpressure in Node.js streams?
answer: Backpressure occurs when a Readable stream produces data faster than the Writable destination can consume it. Without handling it, data piles up in memory. Node.js streams handle this by having Writable.write() return false when its internal buffer is full — the Readable should pause. When the buffer drains, a 'drain' event fires and reading resumes. pipe() handles this automatically.
hint: When the writer can't keep up with the reader — pipe() handles it automatically.

options.option: A technique to compress stream data before sending it over the network
When the writer can't keep up — stream pauses reading until buffer drains
options.comment: pipe() and pipeline() handle backpressure for you — manual stream code must respect it.

options.option: The process of buffering all stream data in memory before processing

question: What is a Transform stream in Node.js?
answer: A Transform stream is both Readable and Writable — it takes input, modifies it, and outputs the result. Common examples: zlib.createGzip() compresses data in-stream, crypto.createCipher() encrypts in-stream. You can chain them with pipe: readStream.pipe(gzip).pipe(writeStream) to compress a file on the fly.
hint: A stream that reads, transforms, and writes — compression and encryption use Transform streams.

options.option: A stream that converts data types from JSON to CSV format on disk
A Readable+Writable stream that transforms data as it passes through
options.comment: readStream.pipe(gzip).pipe(writeStream) pipes through a transform to compress on the fly.

options.option: A stream that maps one file format to another using a configuration schema
A network proxy stream that routes data between two TCP connections

question: Why is stream.pipeline() preferred over pipe() in modern Node.js?
answer: stream.pipeline(src, ...transforms, dest, callback) correctly handles error propagation and stream cleanup. With pipe(), if an error occurs mid-stream, the destination stream is NOT automatically closed — causing memory leaks or file corruption. pipeline() closes all streams on error and calls the callback with the error. It also supports async/await via stream.promises.pipeline().
hint: pipeline() properly cleans up all streams on error — pipe() leaks on errors.

options.option: pipeline() is faster — it uses native C++ bindings unlike pipe()
pipeline() cleans up all streams on error — pipe() leaks on stream errors

pipeline() supports only Writable streams; pipe() works with all stream types

question: What is the 'error' event on EventEmitter and why must it always be handled?
answer: The 'error' event is special in Node.js EventEmitter — if it is emitted and there is no listener registered for it, Node.js throws the error as an uncaught exception, crashing the process. Always register a listener: emitter.on('error', err => console.error(err)) — especially on streams, net sockets, and HTTP servers.

question: What does EventEmitter.setMaxListeners() control?
answer: By default, Node.js emits a warning if more than 10 listeners are added to a single event — this is a memory leak detection hint. setMaxListeners(n) changes that threshold for a specific emitter. If you legitimately have more than 10 listeners (e.g. a library), increase the limit to suppress the warning — but always investigate whether it's a real leak first.
hint: Adjusts the 10-listener warning threshold — a high count often signals a memory leak.

Adjusts the 10-listener warning threshold to suppress false-positive warnings
options.comment: The warning is a memory-leak hint — a listener added in a loop is the typical leak pattern.

options.option: Limits how many events per second an emitter can fire before throttling

question: Describe a real use case for Node.js streams and why they are better than loading data into memory.
answer: Use case: A server endpoint that exports a large database result set as a CSV file. Without streams, you'd query all rows into a JavaScript array, build the full CSV string, then send it — using as much memory as the result set, and making the client wait for the full build before the first byte arrives. With streams: const dbStream = db.queryStream('SELECT * FROM orders'); const csvTransform = new Transform({ transform(row, enc, cb) { cb(null, toCSVLine(row)); } }); res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'text/csv'); dbStream.pipe(csvTransform).pipe(res). Now: memory stays constant regardless of result set size, the client starts receiving data immediately, and if the client disconnects, the stream closes automatically. The same pattern applies to reading files, transcoding video, and extracting zip archives — any time data is larger than available RAM or latency matters.
hint: Stream large data (CSV export, video, file download) to keep memory constant and start fast.

question: What is process.env in Node.js?
answer: process.env is an object that holds all environment variables available to the Node.js process. These come from the operating system or from a .env file loaded by a package like dotenv. Common uses: process.env.NODE_ENV ('development'/'production'), process.env.PORT, process.env.DATABASE_URL.

A module for managing virtual environments in Node.js projects

question: What does process.argv contain?
answer: process.argv is an array of command-line arguments. argv[0] is the path to node, argv[1] is the path to the script, and argv[2] onward are the actual arguments you passed: node script.js hello world gives process.argv = ['node', '/path/script.js', 'hello', 'world'].

question: What is the difference between child_process.exec() and child_process.spawn()?
answer: exec() runs a shell command, buffers the entire stdout/stderr output in memory, and delivers it in a callback — good for short commands with small output: exec('ls -la', callback). spawn() streams stdout/stderr as they are produced — good for long-running processes or large outputs: spawn('ffmpeg', ['-i', 'in.mp4', 'out.mp4']). spawn() is also safer as it doesn't pass through a shell by default.
hint: exec() buffers output (small commands); spawn() streams output (large/long-running).

options.comment: spawn is preferred when you need to process output incrementally or the output is large.

question: What does child_process.fork() do and when should you use it?
answer: fork() spawns a new Node.js process running a specific module and sets up an IPC (inter-process communication) channel between parent and child. The parent and child can exchange messages with .send() and .on('message'). Use it to offload CPU-intensive work without blocking the main event loop — each fork is a full Node.js process with its own V8 instance.
hint: Spawns a new Node.js process with IPC — offload CPU work without blocking the main thread.

Spawns a new Node.js process with IPC — use for CPU-heavy work
options.comment: Each fork is heavy (new V8 instance). For lighter concurrency, use worker_threads instead.

options.option: Forks the git repository inside the project for version tracking purposes
Runs the same script in parallel across all CPU cores automatically

question: What is the worker_threads module in Node.js?
answer: worker_threads allows running JavaScript in parallel threads within the same Node.js process. Unlike child processes (fork), workers share memory (via SharedArrayBuffer) and are much lighter. Use workers for CPU-bound tasks like image resizing, data parsing, or cryptography — tasks that would otherwise block the event loop. Workers communicate via postMessage() / parentPort.on('message').
hint: Lightweight threads within Node.js — share memory, for CPU work. Lighter than child_process.fork.

options.option: A tool that manages a pool of HTTP worker processes for load balancing

question: What does process.exit() do and when should you use it?
answer: process.exit(code) immediately terminates the Node.js process. Exit code 0 means success; non-zero means an error. It bypasses any async cleanup, pending callbacks, or open connections. Use it in CLI scripts to signal success/failure. Avoid it in servers — use graceful shutdown (draining connections first) instead.
hint: Immediately terminates the process. Exit 0 = success, non-zero = error. Avoid in servers.

Immediately terminates the process — code 0=success, non-zero=error
options.comment: Servers should handle SIGTERM and drain connections before exiting, not call process.exit() directly.

options.option: Exits the current function and returns control to the event loop
Stops accepting new connections but lets current requests complete first

question: How do you handle SIGTERM in a Node.js server for graceful shutdown?
answer: Listen for the SIGTERM signal (sent by Docker, Kubernetes, or PM2 when stopping a service) and perform cleanup before exiting: process.on('SIGTERM', () => { server.close(() => { db.end(); process.exit(0); }); }). server.close() stops accepting new requests and waits for existing ones to finish. This avoids dropping in-flight requests during deployment.

question: Explain the difference between CPU-bound and I/O-bound tasks, and how Node.js handles each.
answer: I/O-bound tasks wait on external resources: file reads, database queries, HTTP calls. Node.js handles them efficiently with its single-threaded event loop — while waiting for I/O, the thread runs other work. CPU-bound tasks keep the CPU constantly busy: image processing, crypto, JSON parsing of huge files, ML inference. These block the event loop — all other requests stall until the CPU work finishes. Solutions for CPU-bound work in Node.js: (1) worker_threads — run the work in a separate thread, communicate via messages. (2) child_process.fork() — run in a separate process. (3) Cluster — fork worker processes to use all CPU cores. (4) Delegate to a separate microservice. The key principle: the event loop thread should never be doing heavy computation — offload it so the server stays responsive.
hint: I/O-bound = event loop handles it fine. CPU-bound = blocks loop — use worker_threads or fork.

question: What is package-lock.json and why should it be committed to git?
answer: package-lock.json is an auto-generated file that locks the exact versions of every installed dependency and its sub-dependencies. Committing it ensures every developer and CI environment installs the exact same dependency tree. Without it, npm install may install newer patch/minor versions that could introduce bugs.
hint: Locks exact dep versions for reproducible installs — always commit it to git.

options.option: A file you manually maintain to pin the versions of critical dependencies
Auto-generated file locking exact dep versions — ensures reproducible installs
options.comment: npm ci reads package-lock.json strictly. npm install may update it with new versions.

options.option: A log file showing which packages were recently updated by npm
A file that prevents package.json from being changed by npm commands

question: What is the difference between npm install and npm ci?
answer: npm install reads package.json and installs/updates dependencies, potentially modifying package-lock.json. npm ci (clean install) reads package-lock.json strictly, deletes node_modules first, and fails if lock file and package.json are out of sync. Use npm ci in CI/CD pipelines for reproducible, faster installs.
hint: npm ci is strict (reads lock file, fails on mismatch) — use in CI pipelines for reproducibility.

options.option: They are the same — ci is an alias for install with verbose output
npm ci strictly reads the lock file and fails on any mismatch — for CI use
options.comment: npm ci also deletes node_modules before installing — ensures a clean reproducible build.

options.option: npm ci installs only production dependencies, skipping devDependencies
npm install is faster; npm ci checks for security vulnerabilities before installing

question: What does the dotenv package do in Node.js?
answer: dotenv loads environment variables from a .env file into process.env. You call require('dotenv').config() at the top of your entry file. This lets you define PORT=3000, DATABASE_URL=... in a .env file locally without hardcoding values. The .env file must be added to .gitignore — never commit secrets to git.
hint: Loads .env file into process.env at startup — keep secrets out of code and out of git.

options.option: A module that encrypts environment variables before storing them on disk
Loads a .env file into process.env at startup — keeps secrets out of code

question: What is semantic versioning (semver) and how does it apply to npm packages?
answer: Semantic versioning uses MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH format: MAJOR = breaking change, MINOR = new feature (backward compatible), PATCH = bug fix. In package.json: ^ (caret) allows minor and patch updates (^1.2.3 → any 1.x.x), ~ (tilde) allows only patch updates (~1.2.3 → 1.2.x). Exact version (no prefix) locks to that exact version.
hint: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH — ^ allows minor+patch updates, ~ allows patch only.

options.option: A versioning system where higher numbers always mean more features
MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH — ^ allows minor+patch, ~ allows patch updates only
options.comment: Breaking changes must bump MAJOR. This contract lets npm safely update patch/minor versions.

options.option: A system where packages are ordered alphabetically in the npm registry
A versioning approach that uses dates instead of numbers (e.g. 2024.01.15)

question: What are npm scripts and how do you define and run them?
answer: npm scripts are shell commands defined in the scripts section of package.json. Run them with npm run <name>. Special scripts start, test, and stop run without the 'run' keyword. Use them to standardize tasks: start: 'node dist/main.js', dev: 'nodemon src/main.ts', build: 'tsc', test: 'jest'.

question: What is PM2 and why do teams use it to run Node.js in production?
answer: PM2 is a process manager for Node.js applications. It keeps the app running after crashes (auto-restart), starts the app automatically after a system reboot, allows running multiple instances in cluster mode to use all CPU cores, and provides built-in log management. Run with: pm2 start app.js --name api -i max (max = one instance per CPU core).
hint: Auto-restart on crash, cluster mode for all CPU cores, log management — production daemon.

options.option: A JavaScript testing framework that mocks external APIs for unit tests

options.comment: pm2 start app.js -i max runs one process per CPU core, multiplying throughput.

options.option: A package manager similar to npm but designed for enterprise deployments

question: What should a Node.js Dockerfile include to produce a lean, secure image?
answer: A lean Node.js Dockerfile: (1) Use node:20-alpine as base (small Alpine Linux image). (2) Set WORKDIR /app. (3) COPY package*.json ./ then RUN npm ci --production to install only production deps in a separate layer. (4) COPY . . to add source. (5) Set a non-root USER for security. (6) Expose the port and set CMD. Use a .dockerignore to exclude node_modules and .env from the build context.
hint: node:alpine + npm ci --production + non-root USER + .dockerignore = lean secure image.

options.option: Install all dependencies with npm install and copy the full project directory

options.option: Use node:latest as the base to ensure all newest features are available

question: Explain the Node.js deployment strategy using environment variables and why secrets must never be in code.
answer: Environment variables separate configuration from code — the same build artifact can run in dev, staging, and production with different behavior by changing env vars, not the code. Secrets (API keys, DB passwords, JWT secrets) in code are: (1) committed to git and exposed to anyone with repo access, (2) visible in compiled bundles or Docker images pushed to registries, (3) impossible to rotate without a code change. Proper secret management: locally use a .env file (gitignored) + dotenv. In production use the platform's secret store: AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, Kubernetes Secrets, or Heroku Config Vars. The app reads via process.env at startup. Best practices: validate required env vars at startup (fail fast if missing), use different secrets per environment, rotate regularly, and never log process.env in production.
hint: Env vars separate config from code — secrets in code get committed to git and leak.

question: How do you start a Node.js application in debug mode?
answer: Run node --inspect app.js (or --inspect-brk to break at the first line). This starts the V8 inspector on ws://127.0.0.1:9229. Open Chrome DevTools, go to chrome://inspect, and click your Node.js target to attach. VS Code also has built-in support — set a launch.json with type: 'node' and it attaches automatically.
hint: node --inspect app.js → attach Chrome DevTools at chrome://inspect or VS Code debugger.

options.option: Use console.debug() instead of console.log() to enable debug output

question: What is Jest and how does it work in Node.js projects?
answer: Jest is a JavaScript testing framework by Meta, widely used with Node.js and TypeScript. It provides: test runner, assertions (expect), mocking (jest.fn(), jest.mock()), code coverage, and snapshot testing — all in one package. Run tests with jest (or npm test). Test files match *.test.ts / *.spec.ts patterns.

question: What does jest.mock() do and when should you use it?
answer: jest.mock('module') replaces the real module with an auto-mocked version — all exported functions become jest.fn() stubs. Use it to isolate the unit under test from: database calls, HTTP requests, file system operations, or third-party services. This makes tests fast, deterministic, and not reliant on external systems.

question: What is Supertest and how is it used to test Express APIs?
answer: Supertest wraps your Express app and lets you make HTTP requests to it in tests without starting a real server: const res = await request(app).get('/users').expect(200). It returns the response body, status, and headers for assertion. Combine with Jest for full test coverage of your API endpoints including auth, validation, and error cases.
hint: Makes HTTP calls to your Express app in tests — no real server needed. Use with Jest.

options.option: A load testing tool that sends thousands of concurrent requests to your API

options.option: An API client library for making authenticated requests in Node.js

question: What is code coverage and how do you measure it with Jest?
answer: Code coverage measures the percentage of your source code executed during tests — lines, branches, functions, and statements. Run with: jest --coverage. Jest uses Istanbul under the hood and generates an HTML report. Set thresholds in jest.config.ts with coverageThreshold to fail CI if coverage drops below a minimum (e.g. 80%).

question: Describe an effective testing strategy for a Node.js Express API with a database.
answer: A layered strategy: (1) Unit tests — test individual functions (validators, transformers, utility functions) in isolation with mocked dependencies. Fast, no DB required. (2) Integration tests — test service/repository classes with a real test database (Postgres in Docker or SQLite). These verify SQL, ORM logic, and business rules together. (3) API/E2E tests — use Supertest to call actual Express endpoints. Spin up the app connected to a test DB, make real HTTP calls, and assert response status, body, and DB state. Seed known test data before each test and clean up after. Tools: Jest (all layers), Supertest (API), Docker Compose (test DB), factory-bot pattern for seed data. Key principles: tests must be deterministic (no shared state between tests), fast (parallel where possible), and meaningful (test behavior, not implementation details).
hint: Unit (mocked) → integration (real DB) → API (Supertest) — each layer tests a different concern.

question: What is the Node.js cluster module and how does it help scaling?
answer: The cluster module forks the master Node.js process into multiple worker processes — one per CPU core — all sharing the same port. The OS distributes incoming connections across workers. This multiplies throughput on multi-core servers without changing application code. Each worker is an independent process with its own event loop and memory.
hint: Forks a worker per CPU core, all sharing one port — multiplies throughput on multi-core servers.

options.option: Groups Node.js servers into a cluster for centralized log management
Forks one worker per CPU core sharing a port — multiplies throughput
options.comment: PM2 cluster mode (pm2 start app.js -i max) gives you the same benefit without writing cluster code.

options.option: A load balancer that distributes requests across multiple remote servers
A module that runs multiple Node.js versions side-by-side on one machine

question: How do you detect and fix a memory leak in a Node.js application?
answer: Detection: Monitor memory with process.memoryUsage() or Prometheus metrics. If heapUsed grows steadily without leveling off, you have a leak. Profiling: run with node --inspect, attach Chrome DevTools, take heap snapshots at intervals, and compare — objects that keep growing point to the leak. Common causes: event listeners not removed (.on() in a loop without .off()), growing arrays/Maps storing references forever, closures holding references to large objects, caching without eviction. Fix: remove listeners with .removeListener(), use WeakMap/WeakRef for caches, add TTL/max-size to in-memory caches.
hint: Heap snapshot diff in Chrome DevTools — look for growing event listeners or unbounded caches.

options.option: Restart the server every hour using a cron job to reset memory usage
Heap snapshot diff in DevTools — find growing listeners or unbounded caches
options.comment: The --expose-gc flag lets you call global.gc() to force GC between snapshots for cleaner diffs.

options.option: Use the memory module to set a hard limit on heap size per request
Run node --no-cache to disable V8 caching and reduce memory footprint

question: What is the --max-old-space-size flag in Node.js?
answer: Node.js limits the V8 heap size to ~1.5GB by default. The --max-old-space-size=<MB> flag raises or lowers this limit. Example: node --max-old-space-size=4096 app.js sets a 4GB heap. Use it when Node.js crashes with 'JavaScript heap out of memory'. However, it's better to fix the memory leak or use streaming — simply raising the limit just delays the crash.
hint: Sets V8 heap limit in MB — but fix the leak instead of just raising the cap.

options.option: Controls the maximum age of cached HTTP responses in milliseconds
Sets the V8 heap size limit in MB — use to prevent out-of-memory crashes
options.comment: Raising the limit is a temporary workaround. The real fix is streaming large data or fixing leaks.

options.option: Defines the maximum number of old files Node.js keeps in its module cache
Sets the maximum size of the process.env object in kilobytes

question: What is Node.js profiling with --prof and how do you use it?
answer: The --prof flag enables V8's built-in CPU profiler. Run: node --prof app.js then generate load (with autocannon or k6). This creates an isolate-*.log file. Process it with: node --prof-process isolate-*.log > profile.txt. The output shows which functions consumed the most CPU time (ticks). Use it to find hot paths — functions called millions of times or that take too long per call.
hint: node --prof generates V8 tick log; node --prof-process processes it to find hot functions.

options.option: Enables verbose logging of every function call for debugging complex bugs
V8 CPU profiler — tick log shows which functions use the most CPU time
options.comment: Clinic.js (npmjs.com/package/clinic) is a friendlier alternative with flame graph output.

options.option: Profiles memory allocation to detect which objects are consuming the most RAM
Runs the app with profiling hooks that report results to a dashboard URL

question: Describe a strategy to scale a Node.js API from one server to handle 10x traffic.
answer: A multi-layer scaling strategy: (1) Vertical scaling: increase server CPU/RAM. Quick but limited and expensive. (2) Cluster mode: use PM2 or the cluster module to use all CPU cores on one machine — already multiplies by core count. (3) Horizontal scaling: run multiple Node.js instances behind a load balancer (nginx, AWS ALB). Must make the app stateless — no in-memory sessions. (4) Stateless sessions: move sessions to Redis so any instance can serve any request. (5) Database scaling: connection pooling (pg-pool), read replicas for read-heavy workloads, query optimization and indexing. (6) Caching: Redis for hot query results, response caching for repeated identical requests. (7) Async queues: offload slow background jobs to a queue (Bull/BullMQ with Redis) instead of processing inline. (8) CDN: serve static assets from a CDN to cut Node.js traffic entirely. Bottleneck to identify first: use load testing (autocannon, k6) and application performance monitoring (APM) to find where time is actually spent before scaling.
hint: Cluster → stateless + Redis sessions → horizontal + load balancer → DB pool + cache → queue.

question: Which Tailwind class enables flexbox on an element?

question: What is the difference between flex-row and flex-col in Tailwind?
answer: flex-row sets flex-direction: row — children are laid out horizontally (left to right). flex-col sets flex-direction: column — children stack vertically (top to bottom). flex-row is the default direction when you apply flex.

question: How do you center children both horizontally and vertically with Tailwind flex?
answer: Use flex items-center justify-center on the container. items-center sets align-items: center (cross-axis), justify-center sets justify-content: center (main axis). Together they perfectly center children in both axes.

question: What does the Tailwind class flex-1 do?
answer: flex-1 sets flex: 1 1 0% — meaning the item grows and shrinks equally and starts from a base size of 0. It makes the element take up all available space. Useful for making a sidebar fixed-width and letting the main content fill the rest: flex; sidebar w-64; main flex-1.

question: What does flex-wrap do in Tailwind?
answer: flex-wrap sets flex-wrap: wrap — when flex items overflow the container's width, they wrap onto the next line instead of shrinking or overflowing. flex-nowrap (the default) keeps all items on one line. flex-wrap-reverse wraps in reverse order.

question: How do you add equal spacing between flex items in Tailwind?
answer: Use the gap-{n} utility on the flex container. gap-4 adds 16px (1rem) of space between all items in both directions. gap-x-4 controls horizontal spacing only; gap-y-4 controls vertical only. This replaces older approaches of adding margin to individual items.

question: What does grid-cols-3 do in Tailwind CSS?
answer: grid-cols-3 sets grid-template-columns: repeat(3, minmax(0, 1fr)) — creating 3 equal-width columns. Items in the grid automatically fill left to right. Combine with gap-4 for spacing. Use grid-cols-none to remove all column definitions.

question: What does col-span-2 mean in Tailwind CSS?
answer: col-span-2 sets grid-column: span 2 / span 2 on a grid item — it stretches the item to occupy 2 grid columns instead of 1. col-span-full makes an item span all columns. Use it for headers, featured cards, or full-width rows within a grid.

question: How do you use Tailwind to build a 3-column card grid that stacks to 1 column on mobile?
answer: Apply responsive grid classes to the container: grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-3 gap-6. On mobile (default), grid-cols-1 makes each card full-width. At the md breakpoint (768px+), md:grid-cols-3 switches to 3 equal columns. Each card div inside needs no special classes — the grid handles placement automatically. Add gap-6 for consistent spacing between cards. For a 2-column layout at tablet: grid-cols-1 sm:grid-cols-2 lg:grid-cols-3.
hint: grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-3 gap-6 — single column mobile, 3 columns on md+.

question: What are Tailwind's default breakpoints and what screen sizes do they target?
answer: Tailwind's default breakpoints: sm = 640px, md = 768px, lg = 1024px, xl = 1280px, 2xl = 1536px. Each is a min-width media query, so sm: means 'at 640px and above'. There is no xs prefix — styles without a prefix apply to all screen sizes (mobile first).
hint: sm=640, md=768, lg=1024, xl=1280, 2xl=1536 — all are min-width (mobile-first).

question: What does 'mobile-first' mean in the context of Tailwind CSS?
answer: In Tailwind, unprefixed utility classes (like text-sm, hidden, flex) apply to all screen sizes starting from the smallest. Responsive prefixes (sm:, md:, lg:) add styles that activate at that breakpoint and above. You design the mobile layout first using plain classes, then override at larger breakpoints.

question: How would you hide an element on mobile but show it on medium screens and above?
answer: Use hidden md:block. hidden sets display: none at all sizes. md:block overrides at 768px+ and sets display: block. To reverse (visible on mobile, hidden on desktop): block md:hidden.

question: What does Tailwind's container class do?
answer: container sets the element's max-width to match the current breakpoint (e.g. max-width: 640px at sm, 768px at md). On its own it doesn't center itself — add mx-auto to center it horizontally. You can configure a custom container max-width and padding in tailwind.config.js.

question: How do you make text size change at different breakpoints in Tailwind?
answer: Stack responsive prefixes on text size classes: text-sm md:text-base lg:text-xl. On mobile the text is small, at md it's base size, at lg it's extra-large. Any Tailwind utility can be prefixed with a breakpoint, not just typography.

question: How would you build a navigation bar that is a column on mobile and a row on desktop?

question: What is the purpose of the sm: prefix targeting exactly 640px and up?
answer: The sm: prefix generates a CSS min-width media query: @media (min-width: 640px). Styles inside it apply at 640px and wider — targeting small tablets, large phones in landscape, and up. There is no equivalent max-width-only prefix by default — Tailwind breakpoints only go 'upward'.

question: How do you give a div different padding on different screen sizes in Tailwind?
answer: Stack responsive padding utilities: p-4 md:p-8 lg:p-12. On mobile the padding is 16px (p-4), at md it becomes 32px, at lg it's 48px. You can also control sides independently: px-4 md:px-8 for horizontal only. Any spacing utility accepts responsive prefixes.
hint: p-4 md:p-8 lg:p-12 — padding grows at each breakpoint.

options.option: Use a padding() function in tailwind.config.js with a breakpoint map

options.comment: The same pattern works for margin, width, height, font-size — any utility class.

question: Explain Tailwind's mobile-first responsive system and how you would design a hero section for all screen sizes.
answer:
Tailwind's breakpoints are all min-width, meaning styles without a prefix are the mobile baseline and prefixed styles activate upward. Design process — start mobile, layer upward: (1) Mobile: full-width, stacked layout. (2) md+: side-by-side. (3) lg+: larger text, more padding. Hero section example:

Headline

Subtext

CTA
. On mobile: column stack, centered text. On md+: row layout, left-aligned, image beside text. On lg+: larger heading and more vertical padding. This approach requires no custom CSS — all handled with utility classes.
hint: Start with mobile (column, centered) and add md:flex-row, larger text, more padding at each size.

question: How do you change an element's background on hover in Tailwind?
answer: Prefix any utility with hover: to apply it on :hover. Example: bg-blue-500 hover:bg-blue-700 makes the background blue-500 by default and blue-700 when hovered. Add transition to animate smoothly: bg-blue-500 hover:bg-blue-700 transition-colors.

question: What is the focus: variant used for in Tailwind CSS?
answer: focus: applies a utility when the element has keyboard or click focus — matching the :focus CSS pseudo-class. Common use: styling form inputs and buttons on focus for accessibility: border-gray-300 focus:border-blue-500 focus:ring-2 focus:ring-blue-500 focus:outline-none.

question: What is the group and group-hover: pattern in Tailwind?
answer:
Add class group to a parent element. Then use group-hover:classname on any descendant — those classes activate when the parent is hovered, not the element itself. Example: a card that reveals a button on hover:
.

question: What does the peer and peer-focus: pattern do in Tailwind?
answer: Mark a sibling element with class peer. Then on a following sibling element use peer-focus: (or peer-checked:, peer-hover:) — the styles apply based on the state of the peer element. Classic use: floating label inputs — the label moves up when the input is focused: <input class='peer' /><label class='peer-focus:text-blue-600 peer-focus:-translate-y-4'>Email</label>.

question: How do you style disabled form inputs in Tailwind?
answer: Use the disabled: variant: disabled:bg-gray-100 disabled:cursor-not-allowed disabled:opacity-50. These apply when the HTML disabled attribute is present on the element. Combine with focus: and hover: to build full-state input styling.

question: What do the odd: and even: variants do in Tailwind?
answer: odd: and even: apply styles to alternating children using :nth-child(odd) and :nth-child(even) CSS pseudo-classes. Common use: zebra-striped table rows: <tr class='odd:bg-white even:bg-gray-50'>. Applied to children inside a container.

question: How do you style a checkbox when it is checked using Tailwind?
answer: Use the checked: variant on the input itself, or combine peer with peer-checked: on a sibling. Example with peer for a custom toggle: <input type='checkbox' class='peer hidden'><div class='bg-gray-200 peer-checked:bg-blue-500'>. When checked, the sibling div turns blue. The @tailwindcss/forms plugin provides better base checkbox styling.

question: Explain how to build a custom button with full state styling using Tailwind variants.
answer: A production-quality button covers: default, hover, focus, active, and disabled states. Example class string: bg-blue-600 text-white font-semibold px-5 py-2.5 rounded-lg hover:bg-blue-700 focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-blue-500 focus:ring-offset-2 active:bg-blue-800 disabled:opacity-50 disabled:cursor-not-allowed transition-colors duration-150. Breakdown: bg-blue-600 = default fill; hover:bg-blue-700 = darker on hover; focus:ring-2 focus:ring-blue-500 = visible keyboard focus ring (accessibility); focus:ring-offset-2 = gap between ring and button edge; active:bg-blue-800 = pressed state; disabled:opacity-50 disabled:cursor-not-allowed = disabled appearance; transition-colors duration-150 = smooth color transitions. This covers all WCAG accessibility requirements and interaction feedback without a line of custom CSS.
hint: hover: + focus:ring + active: + disabled: + transition-colors — all states without custom CSS.

question: What does @apply do in Tailwind CSS?
answer: @apply is a Tailwind directive used inside CSS files to compose utility classes into a custom CSS class. Example: .btn { @apply px-4 py-2 bg-blue-600 text-white rounded font-semibold; }. This lets you reuse a combination of utilities under a semantic name without repeating them in HTML.
hint: @apply composes utilities into a custom CSS class — .btn { @apply px-4 py-2 bg-blue-600; }

options.option: Applies a Tailwind theme to all elements inside the selector
Composes Tailwind utilities into a custom CSS class in a stylesheet
options.comment: Only use @apply for genuinely reused components — not to avoid writing utility classes.

question: When should you use @apply and when should you avoid it?
answer: Use @apply when a combination of utilities is used across many unrelated HTML files and you can't use a component abstraction (e.g. a shared CSS framework, an email template). Avoid @apply when you have a component system (React, Vue) — just put the classes in the component. @apply produces larger CSS, loses the benefits of utility-first thinking, and makes it harder to see what styles are applied by reading the HTML.
hint: Use @apply for shared CSS without a component system. Avoid it when you have React/Vue components.

options.option: Always use @apply — it keeps HTML clean and CSS centralized
Never use @apply — Tailwind is utility-only and @apply breaks that model
Use @apply for CSS-only projects; avoid when component abstractions exist
options.comment: In React/Vue, just write the classes in the component — @apply adds unnecessary indirection.

options.option: Only use @apply inside @layer components — never in base or utilities

question: What is @layer in Tailwind CSS and what are the three available layers?
answer: @layer tells Tailwind which CSS cascade layer to inject your custom styles into. The three layers: base (resets and element defaults, like h1 styles), components (reusable class patterns like .btn, .card — can be overridden by utilities), utilities (single-purpose utility classes with highest specificity). Correct layer placement ensures utilities can always override component styles.
hint: @layer base = resets, @layer components = .btn/.card, @layer utilities = single-purpose — order matters.

options.option: base, layout, theme — the three Tailwind CSS cascade layers
base (resets), components (patterns), utilities (highest priority)
options.comment: Utilities are last so hover:, focus:, and responsive prefixes always win over component styles.

options.option: foundation, structure, presentation — Tailwind's three semantic layers
global, local, inline — controls the scope of Tailwind class application

question: How do you create a reusable card component style using @apply?
answer: Define it inside @layer components in your CSS file: @layer components { .card { @apply bg-white rounded-xl shadow-md p-6 border border-gray-100; } .card-title { @apply text-lg font-semibold text-gray-900 mb-2; } }. Now you can write <div class='card'> in HTML instead of repeating all utilities. Responsive and state variants still need to be added in HTML.
hint: @layer components { .card { @apply bg-white rounded-xl shadow-md p-6; } }

options.option: Add card: true to the components section in tailwind.config.js

options.comment: Place in @layer components so utilities (md:, hover:) can still override .card styles.

Use the @component directive: @component card { bg-white shadow-md }

question: What is the @layer base directive used for in Tailwind?
answer: @layer base is used to add CSS resets and element-level defaults. Tailwind's Preflight already applies a CSS reset, but @layer base lets you set your own: h1 { @apply text-3xl font-bold; } body { @apply text-gray-900 antialiased; }. Styles here apply globally to all matching elements without a class.
hint: @layer base = global element defaults (h1, body, a) built on top of Preflight.

options.option: Overrides Tailwind's Preflight reset for all elements globally
Sets global element defaults (h1, body, a) using Tailwind utilities
options.comment: Useful for typography defaults in a project with mostly content pages rather than SPAs.

options.option: Defines the color palette and spacing scale for the entire project
Injects base styles before any @import statements in the CSS file

question: Explain the difference between component extraction with @apply and component extraction in a framework like React.
answer: @apply extracts repeated utility combinations into a CSS class: .btn-primary { @apply px-4 py-2 bg-blue-600 text-white rounded; }. Framework component extraction creates a JavaScript component: const Button = ({ children }) => <button className='px-4 py-2 bg-blue-600 text-white rounded'>{children}</button>. Key differences: (1) @apply is static — responsive and state variants must still be written in HTML. Framework components can accept variant props and compute classes dynamically. (2) @apply makes a new CSS abstraction that must be documented. Framework components are discoverable by searching the codebase. (3) @apply can't easily pass state (hover, focus, disabled) as props. (4) Framework components enable conditional styling: className={isPrimary ? 'bg-blue-600' : 'bg-gray-200'}. Recommendation: in any project using React/Vue/Svelte, always prefer framework component extraction. Reserve @apply for global element styles (@layer base) or pure CSS projects with no component system.
hint: @apply = static CSS class; framework component = dynamic, accepts props, composable — prefer components.

question: What is the dark: variant in Tailwind CSS?
answer: The dark: variant applies a utility only when dark mode is active. Example: bg-white dark:bg-gray-900 text-gray-900 dark:text-white. When dark mode triggers (by media query or a class), the dark: prefixed styles take effect. This eliminates the need for separate stylesheets or CSS variables for dark mode.
hint: dark:bg-gray-900 — applies only when dark mode is active. Pair with the light default.

options.option: Applies low-contrast styles for users who prefer reduced brightness
Applies utilities only when dark mode is active — media or class based
options.comment: bg-white dark:bg-gray-900 defines both light and dark states on the same element.

A filter variant that applies a dark overlay over images and backgrounds

question: What is the difference between darkMode: 'media' and darkMode: 'class' in Tailwind config?
answer: darkMode: 'media' (the default) uses the prefers-color-scheme CSS media query — dark mode activates based on the OS/browser setting and cannot be toggled by the user within the app. darkMode: 'class' activates dark mode when a dark class is present on the html element — you control it with JavaScript, allowing a toggle button. Most apps that offer a dark mode switch use 'class' strategy.
hint: 'media' = OS controls it; 'class' = you control it with JS by toggling class on <html>.

options.option: They are identical — 'class' is the newer alias for 'media' strategy
'media' = OS-controlled; 'class' = JS-controlled via <html class='dark'>
options.comment: With 'class', add dark to <html> when user toggles and persist in localStorage.

options.option: 'class' uses CSS variables; 'media' uses Tailwind utility classes directly
'media' applies dark styles globally; 'class' applies per-component only

question: How do you implement a dark mode toggle button with Tailwind's class strategy?
answer: Set darkMode: 'class' in tailwind.config.js. Then in JavaScript: document.documentElement.classList.toggle('dark'). To persist the preference: if (localStorage.theme === 'dark') { document.documentElement.classList.add('dark') }. The toggle button: <button onclick="document.documentElement.classList.toggle('dark')">Toggle</button>.
hint: darkMode:'class' in config + document.documentElement.classList.toggle('dark') in JS.

question: How do you style a card that has a white background in light mode and dark gray in dark mode?
answer: Apply both the light and dark variants on the same element: bg-white dark:bg-gray-800 text-gray-900 dark:text-gray-100 border border-gray-200 dark:border-gray-700. No conditionals or JavaScript needed — Tailwind generates both CSS rules and the active one applies based on the dark mode strategy.

question: What Tailwind utilities are typically used for a full dark mode page layout?
answer: A standard dark mode layout uses: <html class='dark'> (when toggled). On the body: bg-white dark:bg-gray-950. For text: text-gray-900 dark:text-gray-100. For cards/panels: bg-white dark:bg-gray-900 border-gray-200 dark:border-gray-700. For muted text: text-gray-600 dark:text-gray-400. Inputs: bg-white dark:bg-gray-800 border-gray-300 dark:border-gray-600.

question: Describe how to build a complete dark mode implementation in a Tailwind project, including persistence.
answer: Step 1 — Config: set darkMode: 'class' in tailwind.config.js. Step 2 — Inline script in <head> to prevent flash: <script>if (localStorage.theme === 'dark' || (!('theme' in localStorage) && window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)').matches)) { document.documentElement.classList.add('dark') }</script>. This must be inline (not deferred) to run before first paint. Step 3 — Toggle button JS: function toggleDark() { document.documentElement.classList.toggle('dark'); localStorage.theme = document.documentElement.classList.contains('dark') ? 'dark' : 'light'; }. Step 4 — Style all components with dark: pairs: bg-white dark:bg-gray-900, text-gray-900 dark:text-white, border-gray-200 dark:border-gray-700. Step 5 — System preference: to respect the user's OS without forcing a manual toggle, set media queries or check matchMedia on load. This approach gives: no flash on reload, respects OS preference as default, user can override and their choice persists in localStorage.
hint: darkMode:'class' + inline <head> script (no flash) + toggle + localStorage + dark: utilities.

question: What is the theme section in tailwind.config.js used for?
answer: The theme section defines your design tokens — colors, spacing, fonts, breakpoints, shadows, border-radius, and more. It controls every value that Tailwind's utility classes are generated from. You can replace the entire default scale or extend it with additional values using the extend key.
hint: Defines all design tokens (colors, spacing, fonts) that Tailwind generates utilities from.

question: What is the difference between theme and theme.extend in tailwind.config.js?
answer: Setting a key directly under theme (e.g. theme.colors) replaces the entire default scale — you lose all built-in colors like gray, blue, red. Setting it under theme.extend (e.g. theme.extend.colors) adds your values alongside the defaults without removing any. Always use extend unless you intentionally want to replace the defaults.
hint: theme.colors replaces all defaults; theme.extend.colors adds to them. Use extend.

question: How do you add a custom brand color to Tailwind so you can use classes like bg-brand-500?
answer: In tailwind.config.js: theme: { extend: { colors: { brand: { 50: '#fff1f0', 100: '#ffe4e1', 500: '#F05032', 600: '#d94427', 900: '#7f1d1d' } } } }. Then use bg-brand-500, text-brand-600, border-brand-50 etc. directly. Define a full shade scale (50–900) so all state variants look consistent.

question: How do you add a custom font family to Tailwind CSS?
answer: In tailwind.config.js: theme: { extend: { fontFamily: { display: ['Inter', 'system-ui', 'sans-serif'] } } }. Then use font-display in HTML. Don't forget to also load the font — link it from Google Fonts or define a @font-face in CSS. The font family array is the CSS font-family stack (first available font wins).

question: How do you add a custom breakpoint (e.g. '3xl' at 1920px) to Tailwind?
answer: In tailwind.config.js: theme: { extend: { screens: { '3xl': '1920px' } } }. Then use it like any built-in breakpoint: 3xl:grid-cols-5. The value is a min-width by default. For a max-width breakpoint: screens: { 'max-md': { max: '767px' } }.

question: How do you reference a theme value from another theme key using a function in Tailwind config?
answer: The theme key accepts a function that receives a theme() helper: theme: ({ theme }) => ({ spacing: { ...theme('spacing'), header: '64px' } }). More commonly, use the theme() function inside values to reference another key: boxShadow: { brand: '0 2px 8px ' + theme('colors.brand.500') }. This creates token relationships across your design system.

question: Explain how to build a complete design token system in tailwind.config.js for a brand.
answer: A complete design token system in Tailwind: (1) Colors: extend with a full brand shade scale (50-900) and semantic aliases (theme.extend.colors: { primary: colors.brand[500], danger: colors.red[600] }). (2) Typography: fontFamily for display/body/mono, fontSize with line-height pairs (theme.extend.fontSize: { '2xs': ['0.625rem', '0.875rem'] }). (3) Spacing: extend with named sizes (sidebar: '280px', header: '64px'). (4) Shadows: brand shadow using brand color. (5) Border radius: brand-pill for pill buttons. (6) Screens: add 3xl if needed. (7) Use CSS variables for runtime theming — define in @layer base, reference in Tailwind config via theme(). Good token design: avoid raw hex strings in HTML, use semantic names (text-primary not text-blue-600), make shade scales consistent so states (hover:text-primary-dark) work. Version control the config as the single source of truth for the design system — designers and developers reference the same values.
hint: Colors → typography → spacing → shadows — semantic aliases over raw values, CSS vars for runtime.

question: What does the Tailwind class transition do?

Use transition-colors for just color properties, transition-transform for transforms only.

question: How do you control transition duration and easing in Tailwind?
answer: duration-{n} sets the transition duration: duration-150 = 150ms, duration-300 = 300ms, duration-500 = 500ms. ease-{type} sets the easing: ease-in, ease-out, ease-in-out (default), ease-linear. Example: transition-colors duration-300 ease-out. Combine with delay-{n} to add a delay.

question: What are the built-in animate-* utilities in Tailwind and what do they do?
answer: Tailwind includes four built-in animations: animate-spin (continuous 360° rotation — loading spinners), animate-ping (scale + fade out like a sonar pulse — live indicators), animate-pulse (fade in/out — skeleton loading screens), animate-bounce (bounce up and down — scroll cues). All loop infinitely. Use motion-reduce:animate-none to respect prefers-reduced-motion.

question: How do you add a custom CSS animation to Tailwind?
